| False positives break user trust. | Probabilistic scoring + dual-threshold minimizes unjustified blocks. |
| Scale: 5 billion devices, billions of URLs. | Models are computed in Google's data-centre; only tiny hashes are pushed to clients. |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Used For | Notes |
|--------|----------------------|----------|-------|
| $P(\text{phish}\vert x)$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-(w^\top x + b)}}$ | Logistic regression output | Sigmoid over linear score. |
| $H(D)$ | $-\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Domain entropy feature | High entropy ⇒ random/auto-generated. |
| $d_{Lev}(s_1, s_2)$ | Min. single-character edits | Brand-spoof detection | Edit distance algorithm. |
| $\text{Precision}$ | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Quality of flagging | Penalizes false alarms. |
| $\text{Recall (TPR)}$ | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Coverage of real phish | Penalizes missed attacks. |
| $F_1$ | $2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Combined metric | KTU favourite for trade-off questions. |
| $\text{Accuracy}$ | $\dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Overall correctness | Misleading on imbalanced data. |
| $\hat{L}_{CE}$ | $-\sum_{i} y_i \log \hat{y_i}$ | Cross-entropy training loss | Used to train all classifiers. |
| $\nabla_\theta J(\theta)$ | $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta}$ | Gradient descent update | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta J$. |
| $\text{PR-AUC}$ | Area under $P$-$R$ curve | Robust metric for imbalance | Preferred over ROC-AUC for phishing. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever the question contains the phrase *"imbalanced dataset"* (phishing vs. legitimate is roughly **1 : 1000**), the expected metric is **PR-AUC / F1-Score**, *not* plain accuracy. Writing "accuracy" in such a question costs full marks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The same architectural pattern used by Safe Browsing is reused in:

- **Gmail Spam / Phishing filters** (BERT-based text classifier).
- **Microsoft SmartScreen** in Edge and Outlook.
- **PayPal's anti-phishing ML stack** (feature: site visual similarity).
- **Banking fraud engines** (URL + behaviour fusion).
- **Anti-Malware endpoint products** (CrowdStrike, Sophos) — ML on PE file bytes.

> [!NOTE]
> The case study demonstrates that **AI is most effective in cyber security when it is combined with (a) high-quality telemetry, (b) human feedback, and (c) an efficient client-side distribution channel**. Pure research-grade models without an engineering distribution system *do not stop phishing campaigns*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Logistic Regression Scoring for a URL

The simplest classifier in the Safe Browsing pipeline is logistic regression. KTU 2024 may ask the student to **derive the probability form and explain its decision boundary**.

**Step 1 — Linear scoring function.**

For a URL feature vector $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^\top$, the model computes a weighted linear score:

$$z = w^\top x + b = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j x_j + b$$

*Logic:* Each feature (URL length, entropy, brand-distance, etc.) contributes a positive or negative weight $w_j$. A bias $b$ shifts the decision boundary. This is the standard affine transformation of inputs.

**Step 2 — Apply the sigmoid activation.**

The raw score $z$ is in $(-\infty, +\infty)$, but we need a probability in $(0, 1)$. The logistic (sigmoid) function is:

$$\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

*Logic:* When $z \to +\infty$, $e^{-z} \to 0$ and $\sigma(z) \to 1$ (phishing). When $z \to -\infty$, $e^{-z} \to \infty$ and $\sigma(z) \to 0$ (legitimate). When $z = 0$, $\sigma(z) = 0.5$ (decision boundary).

**Step 3 — Final classification probability.**

$$\hat{y} = P(\text{phishing} \mid x) = \sigma(w^\top x + b) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-(w^\top x + b)}}$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule with dual thresholds.**

$$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{BLOCK} & \text{if } \hat{y} \geq \tau_{\text{high}} \quad (e.g.,\ 0.90) \\ \text{ALLOW} & \text{if } \hat{y} \leq \tau_{\text{low}} \quad (e.g.,\ 0.05) \\ \text{REVIEW (Human)} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

*Logic:* The middle band is the active-learning pool. This is what makes the system *self-improving* — the model literally grows stronger on the URLs it is least sure about.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shannon Entropy of a Domain (Lexical Feature)

The model uses domain entropy as a feature because auto-generated phishing domains (e.g., `bx82kqp-login-paypal.com`) tend to have **higher entropy** than human-readable ones (`paypal.com`).

**Step 1 — Define the alphabet.**

For a domain $D$ of length $n$, count the frequency of each character $c_i \in \Sigma$.

**Step 2 — Compute empirical probability.**

$$p(c_i) = \dfrac{\text{count}(c_i)}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Compute entropy.**

$$H(D) = -\sum_{c_i \in \Sigma} p(c_i) \cdot \log_2 p(c_i)$$

**Step 4 — Worked numerical example.**

Take the domain `paypal.com`. Characters: `p, a, y, p, a, l, ., c, o, m` — total 10 characters.

- $p(\text{'p'}) = 2/10 = 0.2$
- $p(\text{'a'}) = 2/10 = 0.2$
- $p(\text{'y'}) = 1/10 = 0.1$
- $p(\text{'l'}) = 1/10 = 0.1$
- $p(\text{'.'}) = 1/10 = 0.1$
- $p(\text{'c'}) = 1/10 = 0.1$
- $p(\text{'o'}) = 1/10 = 0.1$
- $p(\text{'m'}) = 1/10 = 0.1$

Plug in:

$$H(\text{paypal.com}) = -[2(0.2)\log_2 0.2 + 6(0.1)\log_2 0.1]$$

$$H = -[0.4 \cdot (-2.322) + 0.6 \cdot (-3.322)] = 0.929 + 1.993 = 2.922\ \text{bits}$$

Now `bx82kqp.com` (8 chars, all unique):
$p(c) = 1/8$ for each of 8 distinct characters.

$$H = -8 \cdot \dfrac{1}{8} \log_2 \dfrac{1}{8} = \log_2 8 = 3.0\ \text{bits}$$

*Logic:* The phishing domain shows **higher entropy** (3.0 vs 2.922). The classifier thus receives a strong numeric signal that this domain looks *machine-generated*.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Confusion-Matrix & F1 Calculation (Sample KTU Numerical)

A Safe Browsing classifier is tested on **10 000 URLs**, of which **100 are real phishing** (the rest are legitimate). The model flags **95 phishing URLs**, of which **5 are false alarms** and **90 are true positives**. Compute Precision, Recall, F1.

**Step 1 — Build the confusion matrix from given numbers.**

- True Positives: $TP = 90$ (correctly flagged phishing).
- False Negatives: $FN = \text{Total phishing} - TP = 100 - 90 = 10$.
- False Positives: $FP = 5$.
- True Negatives: $TN = 10\,000 - 100 - 5 = 9\,895$.

**Step 2 — Precision.**

$$P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{90}{90 + 5} = \dfrac{90}{95} = 0.9474$$

**Step 3 — Recall.**

$$R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{90}{90 + 10} = \dfrac{90}{100} = 0.9000$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score.**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.9474 \cdot 0.9000}{0.9474 + 0.9000}$$

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.8527}{1.8474} = 2 \cdot 0.4616 = 0.9232$$

**Step 5 — Accuracy (for comparison).**

$$\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{\text{Total}} = \dfrac{90 + 9\,895}{10\,000} = 0.9985$$

> [!NOTE]
> Observe that **accuracy is 99.85 %** even though the recall is only **90 %**. This is the classic imbalance trap. If the model had predicted *"everyone is legitimate"*, accuracy would still be 99 %. **F1 is the trustworthy metric here.**

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Layer-to-Algorithm Mapping

| Safe Browsing Layer | Algorithm | Input Type | Output |
|--------------------|-----------|------------|--------|
| URL Lexical analysis | Logistic Regression / GBDT | Character n-grams, entropy | $P_{\text{lex}}$ |
| Host reputation | Random Forest | WHOIS, ASN, IP geo | $P_{\text{host}}$ |
| Page content (DOM) | Gradient Boosted Trees | Tag counts, form attributes | $P_{\text{dom}}$ |
| Visual screenshot | Convolutional Neural Network | $224 \times 224$ RGB image | $P_{\text{visual}}$ |
| Text of page | Transformer (BERT-like) | Tokens of body text | $P_{\text{text}}$ |
| Final ensemble | Weighted average or stacking | $(P_{\text{lex}}, P_{\text{host}}, P_{\text{dom}}, P_{\text{visual}}, P_{\text{text}})$ | $P_{\text{final}}$ |

The **ensemble logic** itself can be written as:

$$P_{\text{final}} = \sum_{k=1}^{5} \alpha_k \cdot P_k, \quad \text{with } \sum_{k=1}^{5} \alpha_k = 1$$

*Logic:* The weights $\alpha_k$ are *learned* on a held-out validation set, not chosen by hand. This is the formal mathematical statement of "model fusion" in phishing detection.

### 3.5 The Adversarial Robustness Argument

A complete KTU answer must acknowledge that **phishers actively try to defeat the model**. Common evasion tactics and the AI counter-measure:

| Evasion | Counter-Algorithm |
|---------|-------------------|
| Add many sub-domains (`paypal.com.secure.login.abc.xyz`) | URL tokenization + position-aware CNN. |
| Use Unicode homoglyphs (`pаypal.com` with Cyrillic 'а') | Unicode normalization (NFKC) + script-detection CNN. |
| Geo-fence the payload (block Google IP) | Crawlers proxy through residential IP pools. |
| Random image backgrounds to fool CNN | Adversarial training with augmentation. |
| Fast-flux DNS | Time-series IP reputation models. |

This is the **adversarial AI loop** that makes Safe Browsing a *living system*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Safe Browsing Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[URL Discovery Layer] --> B[Feature Extraction Engine]
    B --> C[Lexical Feature Vector]
    B --> D[Host Feature Vector]
    B --> E[Content and DOM Vector]
    B --> F[Visual Screenshot Vector]
    C --> G[ML Ensemble Classifier]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Prediction Band}
    H -->|High Confidence Phish| I[Push to Blacklist API]
    H -->|Low Confidence Phish| J[Allow and Skip]
    H -->|Ambiguous Zone| K[Active Learning Pool]
    K --> L[Human Analyst Review]
    L --> M[Labelled Feedback]
    M --> G
    I --> N[Client Browser Warning]
    J --> N
    N --> O[End User Sees Red Warning Screen]
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU 5-mark style):**
The URL Discovery layer feeds raw URLs into the Feature Extraction Engine, which produces four parallel feature vectors (lexical, host, content, visual). These are concatenated and scored by an ML Ensemble Classifier, whose output is routed by a tri-state decision: high-confidence phishing is added to the Safe Browsing Blacklist API; low-confidence pages are allowed; ambiguous pages are sent to a Human Analyst Review pool. The analyst's verdict is fed back into the classifier (active-learning loop), and the final blacklist is pushed to client browsers, where the end user sees the iconic red warning screen.

### 4.2 AI Decision-Threshold Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Computed Score p] --> T1{Is p >= 0.90}
    T1 -->|Yes| BL[Add to Blocklist]
    T1 -->|No| T2{Is p <= 0.05}
    T2 -->|Yes| AL[Mark as Legitimate]
    T2 -->|No| RV[Send to Human Review Pool]
    RV --> FB[Feedback Retrains Model]
    FB --> S
```

### 4.3 Phishing Detection Feature-Map (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Input[Input URL]
        U1[Raw URL String]
    end
    subgraph Lex[Lexical Module]
        U1 --> L1[Length and Dot Count]
        U1 --> L2[Shannon Entropy]
        U1 --> L3[Brand Edit Distance]
    end
    subgraph Host[Host Module]
        U1 --> H1[WHOIS Age]
        U1 --> H2[IP and ASN Reputation]
        U1 --> H3[SSL Issuer Check]
    end
    subgraph Cont[Content Module]
        U1 --> C1[Fetched Page DOM]
        C1 --> C2[Form and Iframe Count]
        C1 --> C3[TFIDF Text Vector]
        C1 --> C4[Screenshot to CNN]
    end
    L1 --> ENS[Ensemble Combiner]
    L2 --> ENS
    L3 --> ENS
    H1 --> ENS
    H2 --> ENS
    H3 --> ENS
    C2 --> ENS
    C3 --> ENS
    C4 --> ENS
    ENS --> OUT[Final Phishing Probability p]
```

### 4.4 Active Learning Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[ML Model] -->|uncertain p in 0.05 to 0.90| Q[Review Queue]
    Q --> H[Human Analyst]
    H -->|label| DB[Labelled Dataset]
    DB -->|retrain| M
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows here form a *closed loop*. This is mathematically a fixed-point iteration: the model is updated until the set of uncertain URLs converges to a small residual pool. KTU loves to ask "why is this called a self-improving system?" — answer: *because the model's labels on the next batch depend on the previous batch's outcomes*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define phishing. How is Google Safe Browsing different from a traditional signature-based antivirus in detecting phishing URLs? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Phishing is a social-engineering attack in which the adversary impersonates a trusted entity to steal credentials or financial data via a malicious URL. Traditional signature-based antivirus relies on *known byte-pattern hashes* of malware binaries; it fails against phishing because phishing pages are typically **legitimate HTML/JS hosted on freshly-registered domains with no binary signature**. Google Safe Browsing, in contrast, uses **AI/ML classifiers** that learn from **lexical, host, content, and visual features** of millions of URLs, and continuously update a *reputation blacklist* pushed to > 5 billion devices. It can therefore flag **never-before-seen phishing URLs** within minutes, which a signature engine cannot. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for contrast on signatures, 1 for ML generalization.)*

**Q2. List any three features used by an AI-based phishing detector to classify a URL. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Lexical feature** — Shannon entropy of the domain, e.g., `H(D) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i`. High entropy indicates auto-generated domains.
2. **Host-based feature** — WHOIS registration age in days; very young domains (< 30 days) are statistically more likely to be phishing.
3. **Content/Visual feature** — Embedding from a CNN applied to a screenshot of the rendered page; the network learns "looks like a bank login" vs. "looks like a fake PayPal page".
*(1 mark each.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Modeled on 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern]**

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **end-to-end architecture** of Google Safe Browsing, clearly describing the role of AI/ML in each layer. Highlight how **ensemble learning** and **active learning** contribute to the system's effectiveness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **logistic regression scoring function** used by the lexical classifier in Safe Browsing. Show how a **dual-threshold decision rule** enables the active-learning loop. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) End-to-End Architecture (7 marks)**

The Safe Browsing pipeline consists of five cooperating layers:

1. **URL Discovery (1 mark):** Continuous web crawling, partner feeds (e.g., APWG, StopBadware), and user reports feed a queue of candidate URLs at a rate of > 10 billion/day. A fetch scheduler prioritizes suspicious hosts based on WHOIS age and prior reputation.

2. **Feature Extraction (1 mark):** Each URL is transformed into a numeric vector spanning four families: (i) **lexical** (URL length, dot count, entropy $H(D)$, brand edit distance $d_{Lev}$), (ii) **host** (WHOIS age, ASN, IP geo, SSL issuer), (iii) **content** (DOM form count, hidden iframe flag, TF-IDF vectors), and (iv) **visual** (CNN embeddings of the rendered screenshot).

3. **ML Ensemble Classification (2 marks):** A family of classifiers — logistic regression, GBDT, DNN, CNN, and character-level Transformer — each emits a phishing probability $P_k$. These are combined by learned weights $\alpha_k$ into a final probability:

$$P_{\text{final}} = \sum_{k=1}^{5} \alpha_k P_k, \quad \sum_{k=1}^{5} \alpha_k = 1$$

**Ensemble learning** reduces variance and decorrelates the errors of individual models — a CNN catches visual spoofs that a GBDT misses, and a GBDT catches lexical tricks that a CNN ignores. The combined score is far more robust against adversarial evasion.

4. **Active Learning / Human Review (1.5 marks):** A **dual-threshold rule** partitions the output space:
- $P_{\text{final}} \geq 0.90$ → automatic blocklist entry.
- $P_{\text{final}} \leq 0.05$ → automatic allow.
- $0.05 < P_{\text{final}} < 0.90$ → routed to human reviewers.

Each human label becomes a fresh training example; the model is retrained every few hours. This **active learning** loop is the engine of continuous improvement — the model grows strongest on the URLs it previously found hardest.

5. **Client-Side Distribution (1.5 marks):** The compiled blacklist is distributed as **32-bit SHA-256 prefixes** of URLs, requiring a full-hash round-trip only on cache miss. This pushes the protection to > 5 billion Chrome / Android / Gmail clients with minimal bandwidth, completing the closed loop.

**(b) Logistic Regression & Dual-Threshold Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** A URL feature vector $x = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)^\top$ is fed into the model. The linear score is $z = w^\top x + b$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The sigmoid activation converts the unbounded score to a probability:

$$\hat{y} = P(\text{phish}\vert x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-(w^\top x + b)}}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Properties: $\hat{y} \to 1$ as $z \to +\infty$, $\hat{y} \to 0$ as $z \to -\infty$, and $\hat{y} = 0.5$ at $z = 0$ (decision boundary).

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** The dual-threshold decision rule is:

$$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{BLOCK} & \text{if } \hat{y} \geq \tau_{\text{high}} \\ \text{ALLOW} & \text{if } \hat{y} \leq \tau_{\text{low}} \\ \text{REVIEW} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

with $\tau_{\text{high}} = 0.90$ and $\tau_{\text{low}} = 0.05$ as illustrative values.

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** **Why this enables active learning:** The middle band $0.05 < \hat{y} < 0.90$ corresponds to URLs where the model's *entropy* is high — i.e., the model is uncertain. Sending these to humans maximally improves the model because each human label adds an *informative* training point. Mathematically, this is approximately the **uncertainty sampling** criterion from active-learning theory, which is known to be near-optimal for binary classification.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** The training objective minimises cross-entropy:

$$\hat{L}_{CE} = -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\right]$$

with weights updated by $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta \hat{L}_{CE}$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **role of feature engineering** in AI-based phishing detection. Compare **lexical features** with **visual (CNN-based) features**, stating one strength and one weakness of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A phishing detector was tested on **5 000 URLs**, of which **50** were actual phishing. It produced **45 true positives, 5 false negatives, 8 false positives**, and the rest true negatives. Compute Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Accuracy. Comment on **which metric is most appropriate** for this imbalanced dataset and why. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of Feature Engineering (7 marks)**

**Block diagram (3 marks):** Use the diagram in Section 4.3 (Feature-Map Architecture). The diagram should clearly show the URL flowing into four feature modules: Lexical, Host, Content, and Visual. The outputs of all four modules converge into an **Ensemble Combiner**, whose output is the final phishing probability $P_{\text{final}}$.

**Role of feature engineering (2 marks):** A model is only as good as its inputs. Phishing pages can hide in legitimate HTML, so the model must convert heterogeneous signals — strings, IPs, DOM trees, screenshots — into structured numeric features. *Without feature engineering, even the deepest neural network fails because raw URLs are too high-dimensional and too sparse.*

**Lexical vs Visual comparison (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Lexical (URL string) features | Visual (CNN) features |
|--------|------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Strength** | Fast, no fetch required, works on brand-spoof detection via edit distance. | Detects *visual clones* of real banks; immune to URL obfuscation tricks. |
| **Weakness** | Fails when the URL is innocent-looking but the rendered page is fake. | Requires full page fetch and high GPU compute; vulnerable to small pixel-noise adversarial attacks. |

The two families are **complementary** — this is precisely why Safe Browsing uses an **ensemble**.

**(b) Numerical Metrics (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Build the confusion matrix.

- $TP = 45$, $FN = 5$, $FP = 8$, $TN = 5000 - 50 - 8 = 4942$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Precision:

$$P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} = \dfrac{45}{45 + 8} = \dfrac{45}{53} = 0.8491$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Recall:

$$R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} = \dfrac{45}{45 + 5} = \dfrac{45}{50} = 0.9000$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** F1-Score:

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.8491 \cdot 0.9000}{0.8491 + 0.9000}$$

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{0.7642}{1.7491} = 2 \cdot 0.4369 = 0.8739$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Accuracy:

$$\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{N} = \dfrac{45 + 4942}{5000} = \dfrac{4987}{5000} = 0.9974$$

**Step 6 (1.5 marks):** **Comment on metric choice.**
The dataset is **heavily imbalanced** — only 50 of 5 000 (1 %) URLs are phishing. The computed accuracy of 99.74 % is *misleading*: a trivial classifier that labels *every* URL as legitimate would also achieve 99 % accuracy while completely failing the security task. **Precision, Recall, and F1** correctly penalize the missed phishing cases (the 5 false negatives). Among these, **F1 is the most appropriate single number** because it balances false alarms (precision) against missed attacks (recall) — both are expensive in cyber security. **PR-AUC** would be even better for reporting model quality, but F1 is the standard threshold-free summary.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. ❌ Writing only **accuracy** in an imbalanced-data question — *loses 1 mark immediately.*
> 2. ❌ Confusing the **denominator** of Precision ($TP+FP$) with Recall ($TP+FN$) — *most common error; loses 2 marks.*
> 3. ❌ Saying "AI *replaces* human analysts" — it does not. AI *augments* them via the active-learning loop. Wrong framing = 0.5–1 mark deduction.
> 4. ❌ Forgetting the **distribution channel** (SHA-256 prefix) in the architecture question. The system is not "AI alone"; it is "AI + engineering pipeline". Missing this = 1–1.5 marks lost.
> 5. ❌ Stating $H(D)$ without the **log base 2** qualifier — examiners expect "bits" as the unit. Lose 0.5 mark.
> 6. ❌ Spelling "Sigmoid" as "Sigmoigd" or similar — silly but happens; use a spell-check.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bulleted Summary — Read this the night before the exam.**

- **Phishing** is a social-engineering attack delivered via malicious URLs that imitate trusted brands.
- **Google Safe Browsing (GSB)** is a real-time, AI-augmented URL reputation service protecting > 5 billion devices and processing > 10 billion URLs/day.
- The pipeline has **5 layers**: URL discovery → feature extraction → ML classification → active learning → client-side distribution.
- **Lexical features** include URL length, dot count, **Shannon entropy** $H(D) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$, and **Levenshtein / Damerau-Levenshtein distance** to brand strings.
- **Host features** include WHOIS age (< 30 days = risky), IP/ASN reputation, SSL issuer, and DNS blacklists.
- **Content features** include form counts, hidden iframes, TF-IDF text vectors.
- **Visual features** come from a **CNN** applied to a screenshot of the rendered page — detects visual brand clones.
- The **ensemble** combines model outputs as $P_{\text{final}} = \sum_{k} \alpha_k P_k$ with $\sum \alpha_k = 1$.
- The **dual-threshold rule** ($\tau_{\text{high}}$, $\tau_{\text{low}}$) creates a middle band routed to **human reviewers** — this is the **active learning loop**.
- **Logistic regression** scores a URL: $P(\text{phish}\vert x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-(w^\top x + b)}}$.
- **Cross-entropy loss** is used for training: $L_{CE} = -[y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$.
- For **imbalanced data** (phishing : legitimate $\approx$ 1 : 1000), use **Precision, Recall, F1, PR-AUC** — *not* accuracy.
- $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$, $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$, $F_1 = 2PR / (P+R)$.
- The blacklist is distributed as **SHA-256 prefix hashes** for bandwidth efficiency — full hash fetched only on cache miss.
- **Adversarial robustness** is critical: phishers use homoglyphs, fast-flux DNS, geo-fencing, image noise — Safe Browsing counters with Unicode normalization, IP-reputation time series, and adversarial training.
- The case study is the canonical example of *AI in cyber security* for KTU 2024: it links **theory (ML metrics, sigmoid, entropy) to practice (real, deployed, internet-scale system).**
- The 2024 KTU board **loves** these connections — every answer should explicitly bridge the algorithm to the engineering system.

> **End of Case Study 1 Notes — Module 1 — PECCT523 (AI in Cyber Security)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study 2: Using AI to Detect Anomalies in Network Traffic (e.g., DARPA's Cyber Grand Challenge)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT523 - AI IN CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Case Study 2: Using AI to Detect Anomalies in Network Traffic (e.g., DARPA's Cyber Grand Challenge) # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Network Traffic Anomaly Detection (NTAD)** is the process of identifying patterns, behaviors, or data points in computer network traffic that deviate significantly from an established baseline of "normal" behavior, using **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** and **Machine Learning (ML)** algorithms. According to the KTU 2024 PECCT523 syllabus (Module 1, Case Study 2), this paradigm treats intrusion and malicious activity not as a signature-matching problem, but as a **statistical / behavioral outlier detection problem** in high-dimensional feature space. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Exam Ready):** > *"Anomaly-based Network Intrusion Detection is a pattern-recognition task wherein a model is trained on benign traffic features (packet size, inter-arrival time, protocol flags, flow duration, byte distribution) such that any input yielding a reconstruction error, decision score, or probability below a learned threshold is flagged as a potential cyber-threat — without requiring prior knowledge of the attack signature."* **The DARPA Cyber Grand Challenge (CGC)** — held in **August 2016** at the DEF CON conference in Las Vegas — was the **world's first all-machine cyber-defense tournament**. Seven fully autonomous systems (called *Cyber Reasoning Systems*, CRS) were tasked with finding, proving, and patching software vulnerabilities in real-time, **without any human intervention**. | Parameter | Value / Description | |---|---| | Organizer | **DARPA** (Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency, USA) | | Year | **2016** | | Total Prize Pool | **$3.75 million** | | Number of Rounds | **95 qualifying + 96 final rounds** | | Winner | **Mayhem (ForAllSecure)** — $2 million | | Architecture | Fully autonomous reasoning, exploitation, and patching | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **🛒 The Mall Security Analogy** > Imagine you are a security guard at a 24/7 shopping mall. You don't memorize the face of every criminal, but after **years of observation** you have a strong *gut feeling* (statistical model) of what "normal" looks like: > - People walk at **0.5 to 1.5 m/s** > - Crowds peak between **6 PM and 9 PM** > - Customers carry **1–3 bags** on average > > Now suppose a person begins sprinting at **6 m/s** carrying **15 bags** at 3 AM. Your anomaly detector fires! That is exactly how **AI-based Network Anomaly Detection** works — it builds a probabilistic model of "normal traffic" and flags traffic that statistically deviates from that model. > > The **DARPA CGC** took this one step further: the guard had to *catch* the shoplifter, *identify* the stolen item, and *patch the broken door* — all within seconds, and without calling his manager. ## 1.3 The "Why" Behind This Approach Traditional **Signature-Based Intrusion Detection Systems (SIDS)** like Snort or Suricata rely on a database of known attack fingerprints. They fail catastrophically against: 1. **Zero-Day Exploits** — attacks never seen before 2. **Polymorphic Malware** — attacks that mutate their code 3. **Low-and-Slow Attacks** — Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) that blend with normal traffic AI/ML solves this by learning the **invariant statistical properties** of benign traffic rather than memorizing attack patterns. This is a paradigm shift from *knowledge-based* to *behavior-based* defense. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Insight:** Examiners love contrasting *supervised*, *unsupervised*, and *reinforcement* learning here. Memorize the difference, as it is a guaranteed 3-mark question in Part A. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of AI-Based Anomaly Detection ### Pillar 1: Supervised Learning (Binary Classification) The model is trained on **labeled** datasets (e.g., NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017) where each flow is tagged as *Normal* or *Attack*. $$f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$$ where $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{d}$ is the d-dimensional feature vector of a network flow, and $\theta$ are the learned parameters. **Common Algorithms used in the CGC era:** - **Random Forest** — ensemble of decision trees, robust to overfitting - **XGBoost** — gradient-boosted trees, very high accuracy on tabular flow data - **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** with RBF kernel — strong on small datasets - **Deep Neural Networks (DNN)** — for high-dimensional packet payloads ### Pillar 2: Unsupervised Learning (Outlier Detection) The model is trained **without labels**, learning the geometry of normal traffic. $$p(x) \;\text{high} \Rightarrow \text{Normal}, \quad p(x) \;\text{low} \Rightarrow \text{Anomaly}$$ **Key Algorithms:** - **Isolation Forest** — isolates anomalies via random splits (faster paths = anomalies) - **One-Class SVM** — learns a tight boundary around normal data in kernel space - **Autoencoders** — neural network trained to reconstruct $x$ from $x$; high reconstruction error = anomaly - **DBSCAN / LOF** — density-based clustering ### Pillar 3: Reinforcement Learning (RL) — *The CGC Secret Sauce* DARPA's CGC winners (Mayhem, Rubeus, Shellphish) used **RL agents** to: 1. Decide *which* binary to analyze next 2. Choose the *best fuzzing strategy* 3. Decide *when* to patch a vulnerable program The agent learns a policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ maximizing cumulative reward $R$ for actions $a$ in states $s$. $$\pi^* = \arg\max_{\pi} \; \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\!\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^{t} r_t \right]$$ where $\gamma \in [0,1]$ is the **discount factor** balancing immediate vs. future rewards. ## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | Used In | |---|---|---|---| | $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ | Feature vector of a network flow | $d = 41$ (NSL-KDD) | All models | | $\hat{x} = g_\phi(f_\theta(x))$ | Autoencoder reconstruction | Vector in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ | Deep learning | | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{rec}} = \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert_2^{2}$ | Reconstruction loss (MSE) | Joules equivalent (scalar) | Autoencoder | | $\text{score}(x) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(x))}{c(n)}}$ | Isolation Forest anomaly score | Range $[0, 1]$ | IF algorithm | | $p(x) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \lvert \Sigma \rvert^{1/2}} e^{-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^{T}\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)}$ | Gaussian likelihood | Probability density | Statistical model | | $D_{\text{KL}}(P \,\Vert\, Q) = \int p(x)\log \frac{p(x)}{q(x)}\, dx$ | KL Divergence | **Nats** or **bits** | Information-bottleneck | | $\text{IFR} = \frac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP+TN}}$ | Intrusion False Alarm Rate | Ratio in $[0,1]$ | ROC Analysis | | $\text{TPR} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP+FN}}$ | Detection Rate (Recall) | Ratio in $[0,1]$ | ROC Analysis | | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{prec} \cdot \text{rec}}{\text{prec}+\text{rec}}$ | F1-Score | Scalar $\in [0,1]$ | Model evaluation | | $C_{\text{det}} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Detection Accuracy | Scalar $\in [0,1]$ | Classifier eval | | $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a} Q^*(s,a)$ | Optimal RL policy | Action index | CGC agent | | $\gamma$ | RL discount factor | $0 \leq \gamma < 1$ | MDP | | $\epsilon$ | Threshold for anomaly flagging | Tunable hyperparameter | Decision layer | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Notation Trap:** In the Isolation Forest score formula, $E(h(x))$ is the **expected path length** of a sample, and $c(n)$ is the **average path length of an unsuccessful search in a BST** with $n$ samples. Do NOT confuse $h$ (height) with a hypothesis function here. ## 2.3 Why Each Component Matters (Real-World Utility) 1. **Autoencoders** compress packet-header features into a low-dimensional *latent space* and reconstruct them. Attacks create *non-reconstructible* traffic — a powerful zero-day detector. 2. **Isolation Forest** runs in **$O(n \log n)$** time and is **linear in memory**, ideal for **high-throughput backbone links** (10 Gbps+). 3. **RL agents** in CGC reduced the average vulnerability discovery time from **hours (human expert)** to **seconds (autonomous system)** — a **1000x speedup**. 4. **Information-theoretic measures (KL, JS divergence)** detect *concept drift* — the phenomenon where normal traffic slowly evolves and the model must adapt. # 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Autoencoder Anomaly Score The autoencoder is a 2-stage neural network — an **encoder** $f_\theta$ and a **decoder** $g_\phi$. The bottleneck layer forces a compressed representation $z$. ### Step 1 — Define the encoder $$z = f_\theta(x) = \sigma(W_e x + b_e), \quad z \in \mathbb{R}^{k},\; k \ll d$$ **Explanation:** The input flow $x$ (a 41-dim vector in NSL-KDD) is linearly transformed by weight matrix $W_e$ and bias $b_e$, then passed through a non-linear activation $\sigma$ (e.g., ReLU, Sigmoid) to produce a *latent code* $z$ in a $k$-dimensional space. ### Step 2 — Define the decoder $$\hat{x} = g_\phi(z) = \sigma(W_d z + b_d)$$ **Explanation:** The decoder attempts to recover the original input $x$ from the compressed latent $z$. If $z$ contains all the *essential* information, $\hat{x} \approx x$. ### Step 3 — Define the reconstruction loss (Mean Squared Error) $$\mathcal{L}_{\text{rec}}(x) = \frac{1}{d} \sum_{i=1}^{d} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^{2}$$ **Explanation:** This is the per-sample mean squared error. It is minimized during training using backpropagation on benign traffic only. ### Step 4 — Derive the anomaly score $$\text{AnomalyScore}(x) = \mathcal{L}_{\text{rec}}(x) = \frac{1}{d}\,\lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert_{2}^{2}$$ **Explanation:** After training, $\text{AnomalyScore}$ is *low* for normal traffic (good reconstruction) and *high* for anomalies (poor reconstruction). ### Step 5 — Decision rule $$\text{Decision}(x) = \begin{cases} \text{Anomaly} & \text{if } \mathcal{L}_{\text{rec}}(x) > \tau \\ \text{Normal} & \text{if } \mathcal{L}_{\text{rec}}(x) \le \tau \end{cases}$$ **Explanation:** The threshold $\tau$ is set to a high percentile (e.g., 95th or 99th) of the reconstruction error distribution on a clean validation set. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Isolation Forest Anomaly Score The Isolation Forest algorithm works on the intuition that **anomalies are few and different**, so they are isolated by **fewer random splits** in a decision tree. ### Step 1 — Build an isolation tree (iTree) Starting at the root, randomly pick a feature $j$ and a split value $q$ within the range of $x_j$. Recurse on the child node until the sample is isolated or the tree height limit is reached. ### Step 2 — Path length $h(x)$ The path length $h(x)$ is the number of edges traversed from root to the leaf that isolates $x$. Short $h(x)$ → easy to isolate → **anomaly**. ### Step 3 — Average path length in a Binary Search Tree For a BST of $n$ external nodes, the average unsuccessful search path length is given by the harmonic number: $$c(n) = 2 H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n}, \quad H(m) = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \frac{1}{k} \approx \ln(m) + \gamma_E$$ where $\gamma_E \approx 0.5772$ is the **Euler–Mascheroni constant**. ### Step 4 — Final anomaly score $$\text{AnomalyScore}(x) = 2^{-\frac{E[h(x)]}{c(n)}}$$ **Logical interpretation:** - If $E[h(x)] \to 0 \Rightarrow$ score $\to 1$ → **anomaly** - If $E[h(x)] \to c(n) \Rightarrow$ score $\to 0.5$ → **normal** - If $E[h(x)] \to n-1 \Rightarrow$ score $\to 0$ → **in-distribution (safe)** ## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Optimal Bayesian Threshold Given prior probabilities of normal and attack traffic, the **minimum-cost Bayes decision** chooses the class that minimizes expected loss. Let $C_{ij}$ be the cost of deciding class $i$ when truth is class $j$. ### Step 1 — Likelihoods We model both classes as multivariate Gaussians with parameters $(\mu_0, \Sigma_0)$ for normal and $(\mu_1, \Sigma_1)$ for attack. $$p(x \mid y) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \lvert \Sigma_y \rvert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu_y)^{T}\Sigma_y^{-1}(x-\mu_y)\right)$$ ### Step 2 — Posterior (Bayes Rule) $$P(y=1 \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid y=1)\,P(y=1)}{p(x \mid y=1)\,P(y=1) + p(x \mid y=0)\,P(y=0)}$$ ### Step 3 — Decide by minimizing expected risk $$\text{Decide Attack if: } \quad \frac{P(y=1 \mid x)}{P(y=0 \mid x)} > \frac{C_{00}-C_{10}}{C_{11}-C_{01}} \;\equiv\; \tau_{\text{Bayes}}$$ **Explanation:** If we set $C_{10}=C_{01}=0$ and $C_{00}=C_{11}=1$, then $\tau_{\text{Bayes}} = 1$ and the classifier picks the maximum posterior class. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right AI Model | Model Type | Need Labels? | Best For | Limitation | |---|---|---|---| | Random Forest / XGBoost | Yes (supervised) | High accuracy on known attacks | Poor on zero-days | | Autoencoder | No (semi-supervised) | Zero-day detection | Sensitive to concept drift | | Isolation Forest | No (unsupervised) | High-speed backbone traffic | Subtle attacks may be missed | | One-Class SVM | No | Small, clean datasets | $O(n^{3})$ training time | | Deep RL (DQN/PPO) | Reward signal | Adaptive defense / CGC style | Sample-inefficient, unstable | | LSTM / GRU | Yes | Time-series traffic patterns | Computationally expensive | | CNN on packet bytes | Yes | Payload-level malware detection | Adversarial-perturbation vulnerable | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** When a question says *"Compare supervised and unsupervised IDS"*, this is the exact table to reproduce. Memorize the "Need Labels?" column — it is the most-asked distinction. # 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: The AI-Based Network Anomaly Detection Pipeline This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the full data flow from raw network packets to a final security verdict, reflecting how DARPA's CGC-style systems operate. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[RAW NETWORK TRAFFIC
Packets and Flows] --> B[PACKET CAPTURE
Libpcap, Zeek, NFStream] B --> C[FEATURE EXTRACTION
Flow Stats, Headers, Timing] C --> D[FEATURE VECTOR
x in R^d] D --> E[AI DETECTION ENGINE
Random Forest Autoencoder Isolation Forest] E --> F[ANOMALY SCORE
Reconstruction Error Path Length Probability] F --> G{THRESHOLD CHECK
Score greater than tau} G -- YES --> H[ALERT RAISED
SOC Dashboard] G -- NO --> I[ALLOW TRAFFIC
Log as Benign] H --> J[REINFORCEMENT LEARNING AGENT
Adaptive Response] J --> K[PATCH AND ISOLATE
Autonomous Mitigation] K --> L[FEEDBACK TO MODEL
Online Learning] L --> E ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2: The DARPA Cyber Grand Challenge Architecture This block diagram represents the **Cyber Reasoning System (CRS)** architecture used in CGC 2016. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT_LAYER[INPUT LAYER] P1[BINARY PROGRAM 1] P2[BINARY PROGRAM 2] P3[BINARY PROGRAM N] end subgraph REASONING[REASONING MODULE] F1[STATIC ANALYSIS
Symbolic Execution] F2[DYNAMIC ANALYSIS
Fuzzing and Taint Tracking] F3[NEURAL GUIDED PRIORITIZATION] end subgraph AI_CORE[AI DECISION CORE] A1[REINFORCEMENT LEARNING AGENT] A2[DEEP POLICY NETWORK] A3[EXPERIENCE REPLAY BUFFER] end subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT ACTIONS] O1[PROVE VULNERABILITY
Generate PoV] O2[CREATE PATCH
Network Level Defense] O3[RELEASE SECURITY ADVISORY] end P1 --> F1 P2 --> F2 P3 --> F3 F1 --> A1 F2 --> A1 F3 --> A1 A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A1 A1 --> O1 A1 --> O2 A1 --> O3 ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Autoencoder-based Anomaly Detection Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR X[Input Flow
x in R^41] --> ENC[Encoder
f_theta] ENC --> Z[Latent Code
z in R^k] Z --> DEC[Decoder
g_phi] DEC --> XHAT[Reconstructed Flow
hat x] X --> L[Loss Function
hat x minus x squared] XHAT --> L L --> S[Anomaly Score] S --> T{Score greater than tau} T -- YES --> FLG[FLAGGED ANOMALY] T -- NO --> OK[VERIFIED NORMAL] ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4: DARPA CGC Tournament Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[DEF CON 2016
Las Vegas] --> S2[SEVEN CYBER REASONING SYSTEMS] S2 --> S3[Round 1
95 Challenge Sets] S3 --> S4{Successful Exploit and Patch} S4 -- YES --> S5[Score Point
Offense and Defense] S4 -- NO --> S6[Lose Round
Referee Reveals Vulnerability] S5 --> S7[Final Scoreboard] S6 --> S7 S7 --> WIN[Winner: MAYHEM by ForAllSecure
Prize 2 million USD] S7 --> RUNNER[Runner Up: RUBEUS
Prize 1 million USD] S7 --> THIRD[Third: SHELLPHISH
Prize 750K USD] ``` # 📙 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **Define network traffic anomaly detection. How does it differ from signature-based detection?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Network traffic anomaly detection* is the identification of network flows whose statistical behavior deviates significantly from a learned baseline of normal activity, using AI/ML models. It differs from *signature-based detection* in three key ways: 1. **Knowledge Source:** Anomaly detection learns *behavior*; signature detection matches *known patterns* stored in a database. 2. **Zero-Day Capability:** Anomaly detection catches *previously unseen* attacks; signature detection cannot. 3. **False Positive Rate:** Anomaly detection has a higher FPR (5–15%) because legitimate but unusual traffic is often flagged; signature detection has FPR < 1%. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for differentiation)* ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]` **What is the DARPA Cyber Grand Challenge? Mention its year, organizer, and significance.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **DARPA Cyber Grand Challenge (CGC)** was a fully autonomous cyber-security competition held in **August 2016** at the DEF CON conference in Las Vegas, organized by the **Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA), USA**. Seven *Cyber Reasoning Systems* competed to find, prove, and patch software vulnerabilities in real-time **without human intervention**. Its significance lies in demonstrating that **AI and autonomous reasoning** can perform vulnerability discovery, exploitation, and patching at machine speed, paving the way for **self-healing networks** and **autonomous SOCs**. *(1 mark each for year/organizer/significance)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often *describe* AI methods but fail to *evaluate* them. For full 14 marks, every Part B answer must contain: (i) Definition, (ii) Architecture/Method, (iii) Mathematical formulation, (iv) Advantages, (v) Limitations, and (vi) A worked numerical/metric example. Missing (iii) typically costs 4–5 marks. --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1 Choice]` **a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an AI-based Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS). Discuss the role of feature extraction and the choice of supervised vs. unsupervised models. **b)** Derive the anomaly score used in an **Autoencoder-based IDS**. How is the decision threshold $\tau$ chosen in practice? --- #### ✅ Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks** **Architecture of AI-NIDS (3 marks):** The system consists of four sequential blocks: 1. **Packet Capture Module** — uses *libpcap* or *NFStream* to tap into network interfaces. 2. **Feature Extraction Module** — converts raw packets into 41-dimensional flow vectors (e.g., duration, protocol_type, src_bytes, dst_bytes, flag, count, srv_count). 3. **AI Inference Engine** — classifies or scores the flow. 4. **Decision and Alert Module** — raises SOC alerts on anomalies. **Feature Extraction Role (2 marks):** It transforms unstructured, high-bandwidth packet streams into **structured, low-dimensional, ML-friendly** vectors. Choices include **NetFlow v9**, **sFlow**, and **CICFlowMeter**. Without good features, even the best model fails (the *"garbage in, garbage out"* principle). **Supervised vs. Unsupervised Choice (2 marks):** - **Supervised** (Random Forest, XGBoost, DNN) — used when *labeled datasets* like NSL-KDD or CICIDS-2017 are available; gives high accuracy on known attacks. - **Unsupervised** (Autoencoder, Isolation Forest) — used when labels are unavailable or to detect *zero-day* threats; trades a slightly higher FPR for novel attack coverage. **Part (b) — 7 marks** **Derivation of Anomaly Score (5 marks):** The autoencoder has encoder $f_\theta$ and decoder $g_\phi$. For an input flow $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$: $$z = f_\theta(x) = \sigma(W_e x + b_e)$$ $$\hat{x} = g_\phi(z) = \sigma(W_d z + b_d)$$ The reconstruction error (anomaly score) is: $$\text{AnomalyScore}(x) = \frac{1}{d} \sum_{i=1}^{d} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^{2} = \frac{1}{d}\,\lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert_{2}^{2}$$ **Threshold Selection (2 marks):** $\tau$ is chosen as the $(100-\alpha)$-th percentile of reconstruction errors on a *clean validation set*, where $\alpha$ is the desired **tolerable false-positive rate** (commonly $\alpha = 1\%$ or $5\%$). Mathematically: $$\tau = Q_{1-\alpha}\!\left(\{ \mathcal{L}_{\text{rec}}(x_i) \}_{i=1}^{N_{\text{val}}}\right)$$ where $Q_{1-\alpha}$ is the $(1-\alpha)$ empirical quantile. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **a)** Explain the **DARPA Cyber Grand Challenge** in detail. How did Reinforcement Learning contribute to autonomous vulnerability discovery? **b)** With a diagram, explain the working of an **Isolation Forest** algorithm. Derive its anomaly score formula. --- #### ✅ Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks** **DARPA CGC Overview (3 marks):** The CGC was held in **August 2016** at DEF CON. Seven fully autonomous systems competed in a **capture-the-flag** style tournament. Each system had to: 1. Analyze unknown binary programs 2. Discover vulnerabilities (offensive) 3. Generate patches (defensive) 4. Do this in real-time, with no human-in-the-loop **Mayhem (ForAllSecure)** won $2M, **Rubeus (IARPA-affiliated)** won $1M, and **Shellphish (UC Santa Barbara)** won $750K. **Reinforcement Learning in CGC (4 marks):** The CRS used **Deep Q-Networks (DQN)** to decide *which binary to analyze*, *what fuzzing strategy to use*, and *when to switch from analysis to patching*. The MDP was: - **State ($s$):** current set of analyzed binaries, observed crashes, time remaining. - **Action ($a$):** choose next binary / fuzzing input / patch decision. - **Reward ($r$):** $+1$ for finding a unique vulnerability, $+1$ for a successful defense, $-1$ for being exploited. - **Policy:** $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s,a)$, learned via experience replay and a target network (DQN stabilizers). This RL design reduced human-expert time of ~hours to **machine time of ~seconds**. **Part (b) — 7 marks** **Isolation Forest Working (3 marks):** The Isolation Forest builds an ensemble of $T$ isolation trees (iTrees). For each iTree: 1. Randomly select a feature $j$ and a split value $q$ from the range of feature $j$. 2. Split the data into two child nodes. 3. Recurse until each point is isolated or max depth is reached. 4. Record the path length $h(x)$ for each point. The final anomaly score is **averaged over $T$ trees**. **Derivation of Anomaly Score (4 marks):** Average path length in a BST of $n$ external nodes: $$c(n) = 2 H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n}, \quad H(m) = \ln(m) + \gamma_E$$ Final score: $$s(x) = 2^{-\frac{E[h(x)]}{c(n)}}$$ - $s(x) \to 1$ when $E[h(x)] \to 0$ → **strong anomaly** - $s(x) \to 0.5$ when $E[h(x)] \approx c(n)$ → **normal point** - $s(x) \to 0$ when $E[h(x)] \to n-1$ → **in-distribution point** **Worked numerical example (implicit in derivation):** For $n = 256$ samples, $c(256) \approx 2\ln(255) + 0.577 \approx 11.6$. If the observed $E[h(x)] = 3$, then $s(x) = 2^{-3/11.6} \approx 0.83$ — a clear anomaly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Points (Board-Exam Favourite Topics):** - ⭐ **CGC 2016** — year, organizer (DARPA), venue (DEF CON), winner (Mayhem). - ⭐ **Anomaly Detection** = behavior-based, can detect **zero-days**; **Signature Detection** = pattern-based, fails on zero-days. - ⭐ **Autoencoder Score** = $\frac{1}{d}\lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert_2^{2}$; threshold = $(1-\alpha)$ percentile of validation errors. - ⭐ **Isolation Forest Score** = $2^{-E[h(x)]/c(n)}$; shorter path = more anomalous. - ⭐ **Supervised vs Unsupervised** — Supervised needs labels, gives high accuracy on known attacks; Unsupervised is label-free, catches zero-days, has higher FPR. - ⭐ **Reinforcement Learning in CGC** — DQN with experience replay; reward for finding/patching bugs. - ⭐ **Performance Metrics** — Precision, Recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC; KTU loves asking which is best for **imbalanced** datasets (it is **F1-score** and **MCC**). - ⭐ **KL Divergence** is in **nats** (natural log) or **bits** (log base 2) — *do not* confuse the two units. - ⭐ **Threshold $\tau$** is set using the validation set, never the test set, to avoid **data leakage**. - ⭐ **Concept Drift** — normal traffic evolves over time; models must be **periodically retrained**. - ⭐ **Adversarial Robustness** — packet-level AI IDS can be **evaded** by carefully crafted adversarial perturbations; this is an active KTU 2024 research area. - ⭐ **Datasets to memorize:** DARPA 1998/1999, KDD Cup 99, NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017, UNSW-NB15. - ⭐ **Feature count:** NSL-KDD = **41 features** + 1 label + 1 difficulty level = **43 columns**. - ⭐ **Equation trap:** $\text{TPR} \neq \text{Precision}$. TPR = TP/(TP+FN); Precision = TP/(TP+FP). - ⭐ **Bayes decision rule** uses **likelihood ratio > cost ratio** as the optimal threshold. - ⭐ **CGC's real legacy** is the **autonomous SOC** concept and **AI-guided fuzzing** (e.g., AFL++, Syzkaller). > [!WARNING] > **Final Board-Exam Strategy:** When asked to *compare* models, always structure your answer in a **table** with columns: *Model | Training Type | Pros | Cons | Best Use Case*. Examiners award 2 bonus marks for visual clarity.

topics.title: Introduction to Supervised Learning: Concepts and Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT523 - AI IN CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Supervised Learning: Concepts and Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Supervised Learning** is a foundational paradigm of Machine Learning (ML) in which an artificial intelligence model is trained on a *labeled dataset* — meaning every input sample in the training set is paired with a corresponding known output (label or target). The model iteratively learns a mapping function from inputs to outputs such that, when deployed, it can accurately predict the label for new, previously unseen input data.

Mathematically, the goal of supervised learning is to approximate an unknown target function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ using a hypothesis $h_\theta(x)$ parameterized by $\theta$, such that:

$$h_\theta(x) \approx f(x) \quad \forall \; x \in \mathcal{X}$$

where $\mathcal{X}$ is the input feature space and $\mathcal{Y}$ is the output (label) space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Supervised learning is one of the three primary learning paradigms (alongside Unsupervised Learning and Reinforcement Learning) explicitly listed in Module 2 of PECCT523. The terminology *"labeled dataset"*, *"training phase"*, *"testing phase"*, and *"generalization"* are high-frequency exam terms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are teaching a child to recognize fruits. You show the child an apple and say *"This is an apple,"* show a banana and say *"This is a banana"* — many times, with many examples. Eventually, when you hand the child a fruit it has **never seen before**, it can correctly identify it as either an apple or a banana based on the features (color, shape, taste) it learned during the training phase.

**Supervised Learning works exactly this way:**
- The **teacher** = the *labeled training dataset* (input + correct answer)
- The **child** = the *ML model/algorithm*
- The **"new unseen fruit"** = the *test data* the model has never encountered
- The **child's correct answer** = *successful prediction/generalization*

### 1.3 The Two Flavors of Supervised Learning

| Type | Output Nature | Cybersecurity Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Classification** | Discrete categorical label (e.g., "Malware" / "Benign") | Spam email detection, intrusion detection |
| **Regression** | Continuous numerical value | Predicting severity score, anomaly score estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> In cyber security, **Classification** dominates because most defensive decisions (block / allow, malicious / benign) are categorical. **Regression** is used in risk scoring and severity prediction.

### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Mandated Vocabulary)

- **Feature (x):** An individual measurable property of the input (e.g., packet size, port number).
- **Label (y):** The ground-truth output (e.g., "attack" or "normal").
- **Training Set:** The labeled data used to teach the model: $\{(x^{(1)}, y^{(1)}), (x^{(2)}, y^{(2)}), \ldots, (x^{(m)}, y^{(m)})\}$.
- **Hypothesis $h_\theta(x)$:** The model's current best guess of the mapping function.
- **Loss Function $J(\theta)$:** A measure of how wrong the model's predictions are.
- **Generalization:** The model's ability to perform well on *unseen* data.
- **Overfitting:** Model learns training data (including its noise) too well, failing on new data.
- **Underfitting:** Model is too simple to capture the underlying pattern.

### 1.5 Why Supervised Learning for Cyber Security?

Modern cyber threats evolve faster than rule-based signature systems can adapt. Supervised learning enables:
1. **Automated Pattern Recognition** in millions of network packets per second.
2. **Adaptability** to novel attack variants by retraining on newly labeled data.
3. **Reduced Human Analyst Workload** in Security Operations Centers (SOCs).
4. **Quantitative Risk Assessment** through regression-based severity scoring.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow of Supervised Learning (Step-by-Step)

The supervised learning pipeline is a **five-stage process** that must be executed sequentially for a successful cyber defense model:

**Step 1 — Data Collection & Labeling:**
Historical network logs, malware samples, and user activity records are gathered and *manually labeled* by domain experts. The quality of labels directly determines the ceiling of model accuracy (the **"garbage-in, garbage-out"** principle).

**Step 2 — Feature Engineering & Preprocessing:**
Raw data (IPs, timestamps, byte streams) is transformed into numerical feature vectors. This includes:
- **Normalization:** scaling features to a common range.
- **Encoding:** converting categorical variables (e.g., protocol type: TCP/UDP/ICMP) into numbers.
- **Dimensionality Reduction:** using PCA to remove redundant features.

**Step 3 — Model Selection:**
Choose an appropriate algorithm based on the problem type, data size, and interpretability needs (e.g., Decision Tree for explainable IDS, Deep Neural Network for complex malware analysis).

**Step 4 — Training (Parameter Optimization):**
The algorithm iteratively adjusts its internal parameters $\theta$ to minimize the loss function on the training set. This is typically done via **Gradient Descent** or its variants.

**Step 5 — Testing & Deployment:**
The trained model is evaluated on a held-out test set using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. Once validated, it is deployed in production (e.g., a real-time intrusion detection system).

### 2.2 Core Algorithms Covered in KTU Module 2

| Algorithm | Type | Key Mathematical Principle | Cyber Security Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear Regression** | Regression | Least squares minimization | Predicting attack volume |
| **Logistic Regression** | Classification | Sigmoid function + log-loss | Binary malware detection |
| **Decision Tree (ID3/C4.5)** | Classification/Regression | Information gain / Gini impurity | Explainable intrusion rules |
| **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)** | Classification | Euclidean distance voting | Anomaly detection in log data |
| **Naive Bayes** | Classification | Bayes' theorem + independence assumption | Spam/phishing email filtering |
| **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** | Classification | Maximum margin hyperplane | Network traffic classification |
| **Random Forest** | Ensemble Classification | Bagging of decision trees | Robust malware classification |
| **Neural Networks (MLP)** | Both | Universal function approximation | Advanced threat detection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exams, focus on **Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Naive Bayes, and SVM** — these four are the most frequently examined.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Hypothesis (Linear)** | $h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \theta_n x_n$ | Linear regression prediction |
| **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$ | Cost function for regression |
| **Sigmoid Function** | $g(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Maps real values to probability in $[0,1]$ |
| **Logistic Hypothesis** | $h_\theta(x) = g(\theta^T x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}}$ | Probability of class = 1 |
| **Log Loss (Binary Cross-Entropy)** | $J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} [y^{(i)} \log(h_\theta(x^{(i)})) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)}))]$ | Cost function for classification |
| **Gradient Descent Update** | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta)$ | Parameter optimization rule |
| **Bayes' Theorem** | $P(C \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(X)}$ | Naive Bayes classification |
| **Naive Bayes Classifier** | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c} P(C=c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C=c)$ | Class prediction rule |
| **Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum_{c \in C} p(c) \log_2 p(c)$ | Measure of impurity in dataset |
| **Information Gain** | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Used to split nodes in Decision Tree |
| **Gini Impurity** | $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{c} p_c^2$ | Alternative impurity measure for CART |
| **Euclidean Distance (KNN)** | $d(x, x_i) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n} (x_j - x_{i,j})^2}$ | Distance metric for nearest neighbors |
| **SVM Hyperplane** | $w^T x + b = 0$ | Decision boundary equation |
| **Margin Width** | $\text{margin} = \frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$ | Distance between support vectors |
| **Accuracy** | $Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Classification performance |
| **Precision** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions |
| **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives |
| **F1-Score** | $F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When using absolute value in formulas inside the Formula Sheet table, use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation (e.g., $\vert S_v \vert$) — never the raw pipe `|` — to prevent markdown table corruption.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In an enterprise SOC, a supervised model trained on **labeled historical attack data** (e.g., from the KDD Cup 1999 or NSL-KDD datasets) can:
- **Classify** incoming packets as `Normal`, `DoS`, `Probe`, `R2L`, or `U2R` (the 5 NSL-KDD classes).
- **Trigger automated response** workflows (quarantine, alert, log) based on predicted class.
- **Feed SIEM systems** (Splunk, QRadar) with probabilistic risk scores for human analyst review.
- **Adapt over time** as analysts label new threats, enabling *continuous learning*.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive, line-by-line mathematical derivations of the **four most exam-relevant supervised learning algorithms** in the KTU Module 2 syllabus.

---

### 3.1 Derivation: Logistic Regression Cost Function (Log-Loss)

**Goal:** Derive the binary cross-entropy cost function for logistic regression.

**Given:** A training set with $m$ examples, where $x^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and $y^{(i)} \in \{0, 1\}$. The hypothesis is $h_\theta(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}}$.

**Step 1:** We want the model to output $h_\theta(x) \approx 1$ when $y = 1$, and $h_\theta(x) \approx 0$ when $y = 0$.

**Step 2:** Define the cost for a *single* training example. The intuition: penalize the model exponentially if it predicts the wrong class with high confidence.

$$
\text{Cost}(h_\theta(x), y) =
\begin{cases}
-\log(h_\theta(x)) & \text{if } y = 1 \\
-\log(1 - h_\theta(x)) & \text{if } y = 0
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3:** Combine both cases into a single compact expression using the indicator trick. When $y = 1$, the second term vanishes (because $(1-y) = 0$); when $y = 0$, the first term vanishes.

$$
\text{Cost}(h_\theta(x), y) = -y \log(h_\theta(x)) - (1 - y) \log(1 - h_\theta(x))
$$

**Step 4:** Sum the cost over all $m$ training examples and average.

$$
J(\theta) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \text{Cost}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}), y^{(i)})
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the single-example cost into the sum and write the final form.

$$
J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(h_\theta(x^{(i)})) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]
$$

**Step 6 (Why it is convex):** This cost function is *convex*, which guarantees that gradient descent converges to the **global minimum**, unlike the non-convex 0-1 loss.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Gradient Descent Update Rule for Logistic Regression

**Goal:** Find the derivative $\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$ to derive the update rule.

**Step 1:** Start with the cost for one example.

$$
\text{Cost} = -y \log(h_\theta(x)) - (1 - y) \log(1 - h_\theta(x))
$$

**Step 2:** Recall the derivative of the sigmoid: $g'(z) = g(z)(1 - g(z))$. This is a critical identity.

$$
\frac{d}{dz} \left( \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \right) = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^2} = g(z)(1 - g(z))
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the chain rule. Note that $h_\theta(x) = g(\theta^T x)$.

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} h_\theta(x) = h_\theta(x)(1 - h_\theta(x)) \cdot x_j
$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate $-\log(h)$ with respect to $\theta_j$.

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} [-y \log(h)] = -y \cdot \frac{1}{h} \cdot h(1-h) \cdot x_j = -y(1-h) \cdot x_j
$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate $-\log(1-h)$ with respect to $\theta_j$.

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} [-(1-y) \log(1-h)] = -(1-y) \cdot \frac{-1}{1-h} \cdot h(1-h) \cdot x_j = (1-y) h \cdot x_j
$$

**Step 6:** Combine Steps 4 and 5.

$$
\frac{\partial \text{Cost}}{\partial \theta_j} = -y(1-h) x_j + (1-y) h x_j = (h - y) x_j
$$

**Step 7:** Average over $m$ examples to get the full gradient.

$$
\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)}
$$

**Step 8:** Write the **update rule**. The negative sign moves us *down* the cost surface.

$$
\boxed{\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)}}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** (typically $0.001$ to $0.1$).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Naive Bayes Classification Rule

**Goal:** Derive the classification rule $P(C \mid X) \propto P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C)$.

**Step 1:** Bayes' Theorem (axiom of probability theory).

$$
P(C \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(X)}
$$

**Step 2:** For classification, $P(X)$ is constant across all classes, so it acts only as a normalizing constant. Thus:

$$
\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C} P(C \mid X) = \arg\max_{C} P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C)
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **naive independence assumption** — given class $C$, all features $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ are conditionally independent.

$$
P(X \mid C) = P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)
$$

**Step 4:** Combine Steps 2 and 3 to get the final Naive Bayes classifier.

$$
\boxed{\hat{y} = \hat{C} = \arg\max_{C} \; P(C) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)}
$$

**Step 5 (Laplace Smoothing):** To prevent zero probabilities when a feature value never appeared with a class, add a smoothing constant $k = 1$:

$$
P(x_i \mid C) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, C) + 1}{\text{count}(C) + k}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Decision Tree Information Gain (ID3 Algorithm)

**Goal:** Derive the formula for choosing the best attribute to split on at each node.

**Step 1:** Define the **Entropy** of a set $S$ with respect to a binary classification (yes/no, attack/normal):

$$
H(S) = -p_{\text{pos}} \log_2 p_{\text{pos}} - p_{\text{neg}} \log_2 p_{\text{neg}}
$$

where $p_{\text{pos}}$ is the proportion of positive examples in $S$.

**Step 2:** Compute the entropy *after* splitting $S$ on attribute $A$ into subsets $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_v$:

$$
H(S \mid A) = \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)
$$

**Step 3:** The **Information Gain** is the reduction in entropy achieved by splitting on $A$:

$$
\boxed{IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)}
$$

**Step 4 (Algorithmic Rule):** At each node of the tree, **choose the attribute $A^*$ that maximizes the information gain**.

$$
A^* = \arg\max_{A} \; IG(S, A)
$$

**Step 5 (Cyber Example):** In a network intrusion decision tree, attributes like `protocol_type`, `service`, `flag`, `src_bytes` are evaluated. The attribute that yields the highest IG (typically `src_bytes` or `service`) becomes the root node.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Algorithm Selection Guide

| Criterion | Logistic Regression | Decision Tree | Naive Bayes | SVM | KNN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Output Type** | Probability | Class label | Class label | Class label | Class label |
| **Training Speed** | Fast | Fast | Very fast | Slow for large $n$ | Instant (lazy) |
| **Prediction Speed** | Fast | Fast | Very fast | Fast | Slow (must compute all distances) |
| **Handles Non-linearity** | No (use kernel trick) | Yes | Limited | Yes (with kernels) | Yes |
| **Interpretability** | High (coefficients) | Very High (rules) | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Feature Scaling Needed** | Yes | No | No | Yes (critical) | Yes (critical) |
| **Handles Missing Values** | No | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| **Best Cyber Use Case** | Binary threat detection | Explainable IDS rules | Spam/phishing filter | High-dimensional traffic | Anomaly in log data |
| **Overfitting Risk** | Low | High (without pruning) | Low | Medium | Medium |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Supervised Learning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Cyber Data: Network Logs, Malware Samples] --> B[Step 1: Data Labeling by SOC Analysts]
    B --> C[Step 2: Feature Engineering and Preprocessing]
    C --> D[Step 3: Train Test Split 80 20]
    D --> E[Step 4: Model Selection and Training]
    E --> F[Step 5: Evaluation on Test Set]
    F --> G{Performance Acceptable}
    G -- Yes --> H[Deploy to Production SOC]
    G -- No --> I[Tune Hyperparameters or Collect More Data]
    I --> E
    H --> J[Real Time Cyber Threat Detection]
    J --> K[New Threat Data Collected]
    K --> B
```

**Diagram Explanation:** The diagram shows a closed feedback loop. The deployed model in production continuously generates new labeled data (via analyst feedback), which is fed back into Step 1 for retraining. This implements **continuous supervised learning** — a critical concept for adaptive cyber defense.

---

### 4.2 Taxonomy of Supervised Learning Algorithms

```mermaid
graph LR
    SL[Supervised Learning] --> CL[Classification]
    SL --> RG[Regression]
    CL --> LR[Logistic Regression]
    CL --> DT[Decision Tree]
    CL --> NB[Naive Bayes]
    CL --> SVM[Support Vector Machine]
    CL --> KNN[K Nearest Neighbors]
    CL --> RF[Random Forest]
    CL --> NN[Neural Networks]
    RG --> LIN[Linear Regression]
    RG --> POL[Polynomial Regression]
    RF --> DT
    NN --> DNN[Deep Neural Networks]
```

**Diagram Explanation:** This taxonomy is essential for KTU short-answer questions that ask *"List and briefly explain types of supervised learning algorithms."* Note the hierarchical relationship between Random Forest and Decision Tree (Random Forest is an *ensemble* of Decision Trees).

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Intrusion Detection System (IDS) using Supervised Learning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Input Layer]
        NET[Network Traffic Packets]
        LOG[System and User Logs]
    end
    subgraph FE[Feature Extraction Module]
        F1[Packet Size]
        F2[Protocol Type]
        F3[Source Destination IP]
        F4[Connection Duration]
        F5[Flag Status]
    end
    subgraph ML[Supervised ML Engine]
        M1[Trained Classifier: SVM or Random Forest]
        M2[Probability Scoring Module]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output Decision Layer]
        O1{Predicted Class}
        O2[Action: ALLOW or BLOCK or ALERT]
    end
    NET --> F1
    NET --> F2
    NET --> F3
    LOG --> F4
    LOG --> F5
    F1 --> M1
    F2 --> M1
    F3 --> M1
    F4 --> M1
    F5 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
```

**Diagram Explanation:** This block diagram maps a real-world IDS architecture. The Input Layer captures raw data, the Feature Extraction Module converts it into numerical vectors, the ML Engine applies the trained classifier, and the Output Decision Layer takes automated action. **The classification boundary** is the trained model's decision function $h_\theta(x)$ — if $h_\theta(x) > 0.5$ → classify as "Attack" and trigger BLOCK.

---

### 4.4 Confusion Matrix Visualization (Evaluation Module)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Predicted Positive] --> B{Actual Positive}
    A --> C{Actual Negative}
    B -- Yes --> TP[True Positive TP]
    B -- No --> FN[False Negative FN]
    C -- Yes --> FP[False Positive FP]
    C -- No --> TN[True Negative TN]
```

**Diagram Explanation:** This is the foundation of all classification metrics. In cyber security:
- **TP** = Attack correctly detected
- **FN** = Attack missed (most dangerous!)
- **FP** = Normal traffic falsely flagged (annoying but not catastrophic)
- **TN** = Normal traffic correctly passed

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Recall matters more in cyber security:** A False Negative (missed attack) is far more costly than a False Positive (false alarm). Hence, IDS systems are optimized for high **Recall** even at the cost of some Precision.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define supervised learning. What is the role of a labeled dataset in training a supervised model?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Supervised learning is a machine learning paradigm in which a model is trained on a **labeled dataset** — i.e., a set of input-output pairs $\{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{m}$ — to learn a mapping $h_\theta: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$. The labeled dataset serves as the *teacher*: it provides the ground-truth output $y$ for each input $x$, allowing the model to compare its predictions $\hat{y} = h_\theta(x)$ against the true values and adjust its parameters $\theta$ to minimize prediction error. Without labels, the model has no reference signal to learn from, making the labeled dataset the **fundamental prerequisite** of supervised learning.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for labeled data concept, 1 mark for role of labels in parameter optimization.)*

---

| Aspect | Classification | Regression |
|---|---|---|
| **Output** | Discrete categorical label | Continuous numerical value |
| **Question answered** | "Which class?" | "How much?" |
| **Cyber Example** | Classifying an email as "Spam" or "Not Spam" | Predicting the risk score (0–100) of a network vulnerability |

*(1 mark distinction, 1 mark each example.)*

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of a supervised learning system with a neat block diagram. Discuss any **two** applications in cyber security. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A supervised learning system operates in two phases: **Training** and **Inference (Testing/Deployment)**.

**Architecture (Block Diagram description — 3 marks):**

The system consists of the following sequential blocks:

1. **Labeled Dataset Input Block:** Receives pairs of $(x, y)$ where $x$ is the feature vector and $y$ is the class label. For example, $x = $ (packet size, protocol, flag) and $y \in \{$Attack, Normal$\}$.

2. **Preprocessing & Feature Engineering Block:** Handles missing values, encodes categorical features, and normalizes numerical features. This is essential because algorithms like SVM and Logistic Regression are sensitive to feature scale.

3. **Model / Hypothesis Block:** The chosen algorithm (e.g., SVM, Decision Tree) that outputs a prediction $\hat{y} = h_\theta(x)$.

4. **Loss / Cost Function Block:** Computes the error between $\hat{y}$ and $y$. For classification, binary cross-entropy is used; for regression, MSE.

5. **Optimizer Block:** Updates parameters $\theta$ using gradient descent to minimize the loss: $\theta := \theta - \alpha \nabla J(\theta)$.

6. **Output / Prediction Block:** Produces the final predicted class or value.

**Working (2 marks):**
During training, the optimizer iteratively adjusts $\theta$ to minimize $J(\theta)$ on the training set. During inference, the trained model receives new input $x_{\text{new}}$ and produces a prediction $\hat{y}$ without any further parameter updates.

**Two Cyber Security Applications (2 marks):**

1. **Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS):** Classifies each network flow as `Normal`, `DoS`, `Probe`, `R2L`, or `U2R` using features like duration, protocol_type, src_bytes, etc. Algorithms like Random Forest and SVM achieve >99% accuracy on the NSL-KDD dataset.

2. **Phishing Email Detection:** Classifies emails as `Phishing` or `Legitimate` using features such as URL count, presence of HTML forms, sender domain reputation, and keyword frequency. Naive Bayes and Logistic Regression are commonly used.

---

**(b)** Derive the **logistic regression cost function** (binary cross-entropy) and explain why it is preferred over the Mean Squared Error (MSE) for classification tasks. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

We want a cost function that heavily penalizes confident wrong predictions. Consider a single training example.

**Step 1:** Define per-example cost as a piecewise function:

$$
\text{Cost}(h_\theta(x), y) =
\begin{cases}
-\log(h_\theta(x)) & \text{if } y = 1 \\
-\log(1 - h_\theta(x)) & \text{if } y = 0
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 2:** When $y = 1$ and $h_\theta(x) = 1$ (perfect prediction), cost $= -\log(1) = 0$.
When $y = 1$ and $h_\theta(x) \to 0$ (confident wrong), cost $\to \infty$. ✓

**Step 3:** Combine into one equation using the indicator trick:

$$
\text{Cost}(h_\theta(x), y) = -y \log(h_\theta(x)) - (1 - y) \log(1 - h_\theta(x))
$$

**Step 4:** Sum over all $m$ examples and average:

$$
J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(h_\theta(x^{(i)})) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]
$$

**Why Log-Loss is preferred over MSE for classification (2 marks):**

1. **Convexity:** The log-loss cost function is *convex* in $\theta$, guaranteeing convergence to the global minimum via gradient descent. MSE combined with the sigmoid hypothesis produces a **non-convex** surface with multiple local minima.

2. **Probabilistic Interpretation:** Log-loss is derived from Maximum Likelihood Estimation under a Bernoulli distribution, giving it a clear probabilistic meaning. MSE has no such grounding for classification.

3. **Penalty Behavior:** Log-loss applies an *exponentially growing* penalty for confident wrong predictions, which is desirable for security-critical applications.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Naive Bayes classification algorithm** with its mathematical foundation. State and justify the "naive" assumption. Apply it to a small cyber security example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mathematical Foundation (3 marks):**

Naive Bayes is a probabilistic classifier based on **Bayes' Theorem**:

$$
P(C \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(X)}
$$

where:
- $P(C \mid X)$ = posterior probability of class $C$ given features $X$
- $P(X \mid C)$ = likelihood of features given class $C$
- $P(C)$ = prior probability of class $C$
- $P(X)$ = evidence (constant across classes)

**The Naive Assumption (2 marks):**
Naive Bayes assumes **conditional independence** of all features given the class:

$$
P(X \mid C) = P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)
$$

This is "naive" because in real-world cyber data, features are often *correlated* (e.g., packet size and protocol type are not independent). Despite this unrealistic assumption, the algorithm works remarkably well in practice.

**Final Classifier Rule:**

$$
\hat{C} = \arg\max_{C} P(C) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)
$$

**Cyber Security Example (2 marks):**
**Spam Email Detection** — Let $C \in \{$Spam, NotSpam$\}$. Features: $x_1 = $ "contains 'free'" (yes/no), $x_2 = $ "contains 'winner'" (yes/no), $x_3 = $ "has suspicious link" (yes/no).

- Prior: $P(\text{Spam}) = 0.3$, $P(\text{NotSpam}) = 0.7$
- Likelihoods computed from training set, e.g., $P(x_1 = \text{yes} \mid \text{Spam}) = 0.7$

For a new email with $(x_1 = \text{yes}, x_2 = \text{yes}, x_3 = \text{no})$:
- $P(\text{Spam} \mid X) \propto 0.3 \times 0.7 \times 0.6 \times 0.4 = 0.0504$
- $P(\text{NotSpam} \mid X) \propto 0.7 \times 0.1 \times 0.2 \times 0.9 = 0.0126$

Since $0.0504 > 0.0126$, classify as **Spam**.

---

**(b)** Explain the **Decision Tree (ID3) algorithm** using the **Information Gain** criterion. Show how Information Gain is computed at each node with a small example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**ID3 Algorithm Overview (2 marks):**
ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3) builds a decision tree top-down by:
