1. Computing the entropy of the current node's data.
2. For each attribute, computing the Information Gain from splitting on it.
3. Choosing the attribute with the **highest** Information Gain as the split criterion.
4. Recursively applying steps 1–3 to each child subset.
5. Stopping when all examples belong to one class (pure node) or no attributes remain.

**Entropy Formula (1 mark):**

$$
H(S) = -\sum_{c \in C} p_c \log_2 p_c
$$

**Information Gain Formula (2 marks):**

$$
IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)
$$

**Worked Example (2 marks):**

Suppose we have 14 network connections: 9 "Normal", 5 "Attack". Initial entropy:

$$
H(S) = -\frac{9}{14} \log_2 \frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14} \log_2 \frac{5}{14} = 0.940 \text{ bits}
$$

Consider splitting on attribute "Protocol" with two values: TCP (8 Normal, 2 Attack) and UDP (1 Normal, 3 Attack).

- Entropy of TCP subset: $H = -\frac{2}{10} \log_2 \frac{2}{10} - \frac{8}{10} \log_2 \frac{8}{10} = 0.722$
- Entropy of UDP subset: $H = -\frac{3}{4} \log_2 \frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4} \log_2 \frac{1}{4} = 0.811$

Weighted entropy after split:
$$
H(S \mid \text{Protocol}) = \frac{10}{14}(0.722) + \frac{4}{14}(0.811) = 0.515 + 0.232 = 0.747
$$

Information Gain:
$$
IG(S, \text{Protocol}) = 0.940 - 0.747 = 0.193 \text{ bits}
$$

The algorithm compares this with IG of other attributes and picks the highest. Suppose `src_bytes` gives $IG = 0.521$ — it becomes the **root node split**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Supervised Learning Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "supervised" with "unsupervised"** — A student describing *clustering* (K-Means) in a question about supervised learning will lose **all** conceptual marks. Always verify the question asks for *labeled* data.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the loss function** — In derivation questions, students often write the hypothesis $h_\theta(x)$ but **omit the cost function $J(\theta)$**. Both are required for full marks.
>
> 3. **Not stating the "naive" assumption explicitly** — In Naive Bayes questions, simply writing Bayes' theorem without stating the conditional independence assumption forfeits 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing Precision with Recall** — A common error in cyber security contexts. Recall = "of all attacks, how many did we catch?" Precision = "of all alerts, how many were real attacks?" In IDS, **Recall is prioritized** because missed attacks are catastrophic.
>
> 5. **Skipping the learning rate $\alpha$** — When writing the gradient descent update rule, forgetting the $\alpha$ term results in a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 6. **Using raw `|` in formulas** — Always use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (LaTeX) inside markdown tables to avoid rendering errors. The examiner may deduct 0.5 marks for an unreadable formula.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Introduction to Supervised Learning**

- **Core Idea:** Learn a mapping $h_\theta: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ from *labeled* training data.
- **Two Types:** Classification (discrete) and Regression (continuous).
- **Pipeline:** Data Collection → Labeling → Feature Engineering → Train/Test Split → Training → Evaluation → Deployment.
- **Key Algorithms:** Logistic Regression, Decision Tree (ID3/C4.5/CART), Naive Bayes, SVM, KNN, Random Forest, Neural Networks.
- **Logistic Regression:** Uses sigmoid $g(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ and log-loss cost; updated via gradient descent.
- **Naive Bayes:** Based on Bayes' theorem; assumes conditional independence of features; very fast and effective for spam/phishing detection.
- **Decision Tree:** Uses entropy and information gain; ID3 picks the highest-IG attribute at each node.
- **SVM:** Finds the maximum-margin hyperplane $w^T x + b = 0$; margin $= \frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$; effective in high-dimensional spaces.
- **KNN:** Lazy learner; classifies based on majority vote of $K$ nearest neighbors using Euclidean distance.
- **Evaluation Metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score — derived from the confusion matrix.
- **Cyber Preference:** In security applications, **high Recall** is prioritized over Precision to minimize missed attacks (False Negatives).
- **Overfitting Mitigation:** Use cross-validation, regularization, pruning (for trees), and more training data.
- **Standard Datasets in Cyber:** NSL-KDD, CICIDS-2017, UNSW-NB15 — frequently cited in KTU questions.
- **Convexity:** Log-loss is convex; MSE with sigmoid is non-convex → log-loss is preferred for classification.
- **Ensemble Power:** Random Forest (bagging of decision trees) typically outperforms a single tree by reducing variance.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Supervised Learning: Concepts and Algorithms**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Practical Examples of Supervised Learning in Detecting Malware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT523 - AI IN CYBER SECURITY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Practical Examples of Supervised Learning in Detecting Malware # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Supervised Learning in Malware Detection** is a machine learning paradigm where a classification model is trained on a *labelled* dataset consisting of known malware samples (positive class) and benign software samples (negative class) to learn a decision function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$, where $\mathcal{X}$ is the feature space of executable artifacts and $\mathcal{Y} = \{0, 1\}$ denotes the categorical label (malicious or benign). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT523 Module 2):** > "Supervised learning for malware detection involves training a model using labelled samples of malicious and benign code, where the model learns the mapping between extracted features (static or behavioural) and their corresponding class labels. The trained model is then deployed to classify unseen executables." The two principal approaches of feature extraction are: - **Static Analysis** – Features are extracted *without executing* the file (e.g., byte sequences, opcode n-grams, PE header fields, string signatures). - **Dynamic / Behavioural Analysis** – Features are extracted by *executing* the file in a sandbox and observing system calls, API calls, network traffic, and file modifications. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams:** > - **Feature Vector** $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ – numerical representation of a sample. > - **Label** $y \in \{0,1\}$ – ground truth class (0 = benign, 1 = malware). > - **Training Set** $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ – the labelled dataset. > - **Classifier** $h_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$ – the learned hypothesis parameterized by $\theta$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **post office customs officer** who has to decide whether an incoming parcel contains a banned item. Over the years, the officer has inspected **thousands of parcels** – for each one, a senior officer has already told them "this one was dangerous" or "this one was safe." The customs officer begins noticing patterns: - Parcels wrapped in black plastic with no return address → often dangerous. - Parcels with a familiar sender and proper documentation → usually safe. After enough examples, the officer can classify a **new** parcel simply by observing its visible features (weight, smell, label printing, X-ray scan), without opening it. **Supervised learning for malware detection works exactly the same way:** - The **labelled training data** is the officer's past experience. - The **features** (file size, opcode histogram, API call frequency) are the visible parcel attributes. - The **classifier** is the officer's internal decision rule. - A **new, unseen .exe file** is the new parcel arriving for inspection. > [!TIP] > Think of supervised learning as a student preparing for an exam using a textbook that contains *both* the questions *and* the correct answers. At exam time, when a *new* question appears, the student can predict the answer based on patterns memorised from the textbook. ## 1.3 Why Supervised Learning is the Backbone of Modern AV Engines Traditional signature-based antivirus systems rely on exact byte-pattern matching and fail against **polymorphic**, **metamorphic**, and **zero-day** malware. Supervised learning generalises from known examples to detect *previously unseen* variants that share statistical similarity with training samples. The **end-to-end detection pipeline** consists of four stages: 1. **Sample Collection** – harvest benign and malicious executables from repositories (VirusShare, MalShare, EMBER). 2. **Feature Extraction** – convert binaries into numerical vectors using static or dynamic analysis. 3. **Model Training** – fit a classifier (Decision Tree, SVM, Random Forest, Deep Neural Network). 4. **Inference & Deployment** – classify new files in real time with confidence scores. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Malware Detection Pipeline (Operational Logic) The supervised learning workflow for malware detection can be decomposed into seven well-defined steps. Each step has a precise engineering rationale: | Step | Operation | Engineering Purpose | |:-----|:----------|:-------------------| | 1 | **Sample Collection** | Build a representative corpus of benign + malicious executables. | | 2 | **Labelling** | Assign ground truth $y_i \in \{0,1\}$ using authoritative sources (VirusTotal consensus). | | 3 | **Feature Engineering** | Convert raw bytes / behaviour logs into $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$. | | 4 | **Train/Validation Split** | Partition $\mathcal{D}$ into $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$ and $\mathcal{D}_{\text{val}}$ (typically 80/20). | | 5 | **Model Fitting** | Optimise parameters $\theta$ to minimise a loss function $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$. | | 6 | **Hyperparameter Tuning** | Use cross-validation to select optimal model complexity. | | 7 | **Inference** | Deploy $h_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$ to score new, unlabelled executables. | ## 2.2 Feature Engineering – The Most Critical Step > [!IMPORTANT] > "Better features beat better algorithms." — A principle emphasised in KTU 2024 scheme Module 2. ### 2.2.1 Static Features Extracted from the binary file *without execution*: - **PE Header Fields** – `SizeOfImage`, `NumberOfSections`, `AddressOfEntryPoint`. - **Byte-level n-grams** – sequences of $n$ consecutive bytes (e.g., 2-grams, 4-grams). - **Opcode n-grams** – frequency of disassembled instruction sequences. - **String Features** – presence of suspicious URLs, registry keys, mutex names. - **Entropy** – Shannon entropy of the file's bytes (high entropy often indicates packing/encryption). ### 2.2.2 Dynamic Features Extracted by executing the sample in an instrumented sandbox (Cuckoo, ANY.RUN): - **API Call Sequences** – ordered list of Windows API invocations. - **System Call Traces** – `CreateFile`, `WriteProcessMemory`, `SetWindowsHookEx`, etc. - **Network Behaviour** – DNS queries, HTTP POST payloads, IP destinations. - **File System Modifications** – files created, modified, deleted. - **Registry Modifications** – persistence keys (e.g., `Run`, `RunOnce`). ## 2.3 Classification Algorithms Commonly Used | Algorithm | Strength in Malware Detection | Weakness | |:----------|:------------------------------|:---------| | **Decision Tree (J48/C4.5)** | Interpretable rules; useful for triage | Prone to overfitting | | **Random Forest** | Robust to noise; handles high-dim features | Less interpretable | | **SVM (with RBF kernel)** | Effective on small-to-medium datasets | Slow on large corpora | | **k-Nearest Neighbours** | Simple, non-parametric | High inference cost | | **Naïve Bayes** | Fast, works with limited data | Strong independence assumption | | **Deep Neural Networks (CNN/LSTM)** | Excels on raw bytes/sequences | Requires large labelled corpora | | **Gradient Boosting (XGBoost)** | State-of-the-art on tabular features | Hyperparameter sensitive | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Memorise the following equations. They appear regularly in KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. | # | Concept | Formula | Description | |:-:|:--------|:--------|:------------| | 1 | **Binary Cross-Entropy Loss** | $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i\log\hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\right]$ | Loss used for binary classification. | | 2 | **Sigmoid Function** | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Maps real-valued score to probability $\hat{y}\in(0,1)$. | | 3 | **Decision Rule** | $\hat{y} = \mathbb{1}\big[\sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x} + b) \geq 0.5\big]$ | Threshold-based classification. | | 4 | **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Overall correctness. | | 5 | **Precision** | $\text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Of those flagged, how many are truly malicious. | | 6 | **Recall (TPR)** | $\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Of all malware, how many were caught. | | 7 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean of precision & recall. | | 8 | **False Positive Rate** | $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | Benign files wrongly flagged. | | 9 | **ROC-AUC** | $\text{AUC} = \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}^{-1}(t))\,dt$ | Aggregate performance across thresholds. | | 10 | **Shannon Entropy** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Measures randomness of byte distribution. | | 11 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{sim}(\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2) = \frac{\mathbf{x}_1^\top\mathbf{x}_2}{\vert\vert\mathbf{x}_1\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert\mathbf{x}_2\vert\vert}$ | Distance between two feature vectors. | | 12 | **L2 Regularisation** | $\Omega(\theta) = \lambda \vert\vert\theta\vert\vert_2^2$ | Penalty to reduce overfitting. | | 13 | **k-NN Decision Rule** | $\hat{y} = \text{mode}\{y_i \mid \mathbf{x}_i \in \mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x})\}$ | Majority vote of $k$ nearest neighbours. | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Supervised learning for malware detection is deployed in: - **Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)** systems (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne). - **Email Security Gateways** – classifying attachments. - **Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS)** – classifying payload binaries. - **Mobile App Stores** – scanning APKs before publication. - **Sandbox-as-a-Service platforms** – Cuckoo Sandbox + ML classifier ensemble. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Linear Score to Probability (Logistic Regression) > [!NOTE] > Logistic regression is the **canonical** supervised classifier. KTU frequently asks derivations linking the linear model to the sigmoid and the loss function. ### Step 1 — Begin with a Linear Scoring Function The most basic model computes a real-valued score as a weighted sum of features: $$ z = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b $$ Here $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the weight vector, $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the feature vector of a malware sample, and $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the bias term. ### Step 2 — Convert the Score to a Probability Using the Sigmoid We need to squash the unbounded real number $z$ into the interval $(0, 1)$ so that it can be interpreted as the probability that the sample is malicious: $$ \hat{y} = P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} $$ ### Step 3 — Rewrite the Sigmoid in Logit Form (Algebraic Transition) Take the reciprocal of $\hat{y}$: $$ \frac{1}{\hat{y}} = 1 + e^{-z} $$ Subtract 1 and take the natural logarithm: $$ \log\left(\frac{\hat{y}}{1-\hat{y}}\right) = z = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b $$ This expression is called the **log-odds** or **logit** of the event "sample is malicious." It tells us that the log-odds of malware is a *linear* function of the input features. ### Step 4 — Define the Loss Function (Negative Log Likelihood) We now need a loss function that is large when our prediction $\hat{y}$ disagrees with the true label $y$, and small when it agrees. For a single sample, the likelihood under the Bernoulli model is: $$ P(y \mid \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{w}) = \hat{y}^{y}(1-\hat{y})^{(1-y)} $$ Taking the negative logarithm of this likelihood gives a convex loss function suitable for gradient-based optimisation: $$ \ell(\mathbf{w}) = -\big[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\big] $$ ### Step 5 — Aggregate the Loss Over the Training Set The empirical risk (average loss) over $N$ training samples is the **Binary Cross-Entropy** loss: $$ \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Big[y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\Big] $$ ### Step 6 — Gradient Update Rule (Why the Model Learns) Taking the partial derivative of $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to a single weight $w_j$: $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\big(\hat{y}_i - y_i\big)\,x_{ij} $$ The weight is then updated using gradient descent with learning rate $\eta$: $$ w_j \leftarrow w_j - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_j} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** The update is proportional to the *prediction error* $(\hat{y}_i - y_i)$. If we underpredict malware ($\hat{y}_i < y_i$), the weight $w_j$ increases for features $x_{ij}$ that are positively associated with malware. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Deriving the F1-Score from Precision and Recall The F1-Score is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. KTU examiners often ask to derive it from first principles. ### Step 1 — Definition of Harmonic Mean For two positive numbers $a$ and $b$, the harmonic mean is: $$ H(a,b) = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}} $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute $a = P$ (Precision) and $b = R$ (Recall) $$ F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}} = \frac{2PR}{P+R} $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute the Confusion-Matrix Expressions $$ P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}, \quad R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} $$ Plugging in: $$ F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\frac{TP}{TP+FP} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN}} $$ ### Step 4 — Simplify by Common Denominator Multiply numerator and denominator by $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)$: $$ F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP)} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2TP + FP + FN} $$ > [!TIP] > **Why the Harmonic Mean?** The arithmetic mean would be inflated by extreme values, but the harmonic mean *penalises imbalance*. A classifier with Precision=1.0 and Recall=0.1 (catches very few malware, but is highly confident) has $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{1.0 \cdot 0.1}{1.1} \approx 0.18$, reflecting its true weakness. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Naïve Bayes Classifier for Malware Detection Naïve Bayes is a probabilistic classifier based on Bayes' theorem with a strong (naïve) feature independence assumption. ### Step 1 — Apply Bayes' Theorem $$ P(y \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid y)\,P(y)}{P(\mathbf{x})} $$ ### Step 2 — Decide the Class by Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) $$ \hat{y} = \arg\max_{y \in \{0,1\}} P(y \mid \mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{y} P(\mathbf{x} \mid y)\,P(y) $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Naïve Independence Assumption $$ P(\mathbf{x} \mid y) = \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid y) $$ This assumes each feature $x_j$ is conditionally independent of the others given the class label $y$. ### Step 4 — Use Log-Space to Avoid Numerical Underflow Taking the natural logarithm, products become sums: $$ \hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} \Big[\log P(y) + \sum_{j=1}^{d} \log P(x_j \mid y)\Big] $$ ### Step 5 — Practical Estimation (with Laplace Smoothing) For a binary feature $x_j \in \{0,1\}$: $$ P(x_j = 1 \mid y = 1) = \frac{\#\{i: x_{ij}=1 \text{ and } y_i=1\} + \alpha}{\#\{i: y_i=1\} + 2\alpha} $$ where $\alpha = 1$ is the Laplace smoothing parameter that prevents zero probabilities. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical Caveat:** Despite its simplicity, Naïve Bayes performs surprisingly well in spam and malware filtering because the independence assumption, while violated in practice, often yields *good* probability *rankings* rather than calibrated probabilities. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Practical Examples (KTU Favourite) | Practical Example | Feature Type | Recommended Algorithm | Typical F1-Score | |:------------------|:-------------|:----------------------|:----------------| | **PE File Classification (EMBER Dataset)** | Static (PE header + byte histogram) | LightGBM / XGBoost | 0.98 – 0.99 | | **Android Malware Detection (Drebin)** | Static (permissions + API calls) | Linear SVM | 0.93 – 0.96 | | **API Call Sequence Classification** | Dynamic (sequential) | LSTM / 1-D CNN | 0.95 – 0.97 | | **Phishing Email Attachment Scanning** | Mixed (header + body + attachment) | Random Forest | 0.94 – 0.97 | | **Ransomware Behaviour Detection** | Dynamic (file I/O + crypto-API) | Gradient Boosting | 0.97 – 0.99 | | **PDF Malware Identification** | Static (JavaScript + embedded objects) | Decision Tree / RF | 0.92 – 0.95 | | **Network-Based Malware C2 Detection** | Dynamic (flow features) | XGBoost / Deep Learning | 0.93 – 0.96 | > [!TIP] > In the KTU board exam, when asked for a "practical example," always specify **(i) the dataset name, (ii) the feature type, (iii) the algorithm, and (iv) the reported accuracy/F1-score**. This is the structure the model answer template follows. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Supervised Malware Detection Pipeline The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete supervised-learning workflow, from raw binary ingestion to deployed model inference. This is the **block-level functional architecture** most KTU examiners expect when they ask students to "draw and explain the malware detection pipeline." ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Executable File] --> B[Preprocessing Module] B --> C{Analysis Type} C -->|Static| D1[PE Parser / Disassembler] C -->|Dynamic| D2[Sandbox Execution] D1 --> E1[Static Features
Opcode n-grams, Imports, Entropy] D2 --> E2[Dynamic Features
API calls, Network traffic, File ops] E1 --> F[Feature Vector x in Real-D] E2 --> F F --> G[Labelled Dataset Construction
x_i, y_i] G --> H[Train / Validation Split 80-20] H --> I[Classifier Training
SVM, RF, XGBoost, DNN] I --> J[Hyperparameter Tuning
k-fold Cross Validation] J --> K[Performance Evaluation
Acc, Prec, Rec, F1, AUC] K --> L{Quality Gate Met?} L -->|No| M[Re-engineer Features / Tune Model] M --> I L -->|Yes| N[Deploy Trained Model h-theta] N --> O[Inference Engine] P[New Unseen Executable] --> O O --> Q{Predicted Label} Q -->|Malware y=1| R[Quarantine and Alert] Q -->|Benign y=0| S[Allow Execution] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagram:** The left branch (Static) and right branch (Dynamic) are alternative feature-extraction strategies. In production-grade systems (e.g., VirusTotal, CrowdStrike Falcon), **both** branches are typically executed in parallel and their predictions are fused through an ensemble layer for higher robustness. ## 4.2 Decision Boundary Visualisation (Conceptual Schematic) The diagram below illustrates how a trained classifier partitions the feature space into two regions: malware (red) and benign (green). The decision boundary is the geometric locus where the model is indifferent between the two classes. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FS["Feature Space Real-to-the-2"] direction LR R["Region R-malware
h-theta x >= 0.5"] G["Region G-benign
h-theta x less-than 0.5"] DB["Decision Boundary
w transpose x + b = 0"] end R --- DB DB --- G R -.Sample.-> P1["Sample s1: Predicted Malware"] G -.Sample.-> P2["Sample s2: Predicted Benign"] P1 -.Just inside.-> DB P2 -.Just outside.-> DB ``` > [!TIP] > The decision boundary need not be linear. A **kernelised SVM** yields a *non-linear* boundary in the original feature space, while remaining a *hyperplane* in a higher-dimensional implicit space (the "kernel trick"). This is a high-yield KTU exam point. ## 4.3 Confusion-Matrix Heat-Map (Performance Evaluation Schematic) For a KTU Part-B question, you may be asked to interpret a confusion matrix. The following block diagram outlines the four cardinal cells and their security implications. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CM["Confusion Matrix for Malware Detection"] direction TB subgraph ACT["Actual Class"] direction LR A1[Actually Malware y=1] A2[Actually Benign y=0] end subgraph PRED["Predicted Class"] direction LR B1[Predicted Malware y-hat=1] B2[Predicted Benign y-hat=0] end end A1 --> B1 A1 --> B2 A2 --> B1 A2 --> B2 B1 -.TP.-> C1["True Positive TP
Malware correctly caught"] B1 -.FP.-> C2["False Positive FP
Benign wrongly blocked
USER FRUSTRATION"] B2 -.FN.-> C3["False Negative FN
Malware missed
SECURITY BREACH"] B2 -.TN.-> C4["True Negative TN
Benign correctly allowed"] ``` > [!WARNING] > **Security Trade-off:** A *False Negative* (missed malware) is far more dangerous than a *False Positive* (wrongly blocked benign file). Hence, in production, the decision threshold is often **lowered below 0.5** to favour *recall* over precision — accepting more false alarms in exchange for fewer missed infections. ## 4.4 Algorithm Selection Decision Tree (Practical Guidance) This is the **practical decision-support schematic** that KTU examiners love asking: "Which supervised algorithm would you choose for a given scenario, and why?" ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Choose Supervised Algorithm] --> B{Dataset Size} B -->|Small < 10k samples| C{Linear Separable?} B -->|Large > 100k samples| D{Feature Modality} C -->|Yes| E[Logistic Regression or Linear SVM] C -->|No| F[k-NN or RBF Kernel SVM] D -->|Tabular Features| G[Gradient Boosting XGBoost or LightGBM] D -->|Raw Byte Sequences| H[1-D Convolutional Neural Network] D -->|Sequential API Calls| I[Recurrent Network LSTM or GRU] D -->|Image of Binary| J[2-D CNN treating bytes as pixels] E --> K[Evaluate via k-fold Cross Validation] F --> K G --> K H --> K I --> K J --> K K --> L{Production Latency Budget} L -->|Strict less-than 10ms| M[Distill to Decision Tree or Logistic Regression] L -->|Relaxed| N[Deploy Original Model] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > Part A questions are concise, definition-based, and test recall. Answers should be 3–5 sentences and may include a small equation. ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT523]** **"Define supervised learning in the context of malware detection. Give one practical example."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Supervised learning is a machine learning paradigm in which a model is trained on a dataset of samples labelled with their correct class — in malware detection, the two classes are *malicious* and *benign*. The model learns a mapping $h_{\theta}: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$ from feature vectors to class labels by minimising a loss function such as binary cross-entropy. **Practical example:** Classifying Windows PE executables as malicious or benign using the **EMBER dataset** (a corpus of 1.1M samples with static PE-header features) by training a **LightGBM** gradient-boosted decision-tree classifier, which achieves an **F1-score of ~0.98** on held-out test data. *(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for example with metric.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT523]** **"Differentiate between static and dynamic feature extraction in supervised malware detection."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Static Features | Dynamic Features | |:-------|:----------------|:-----------------| | When extracted | *Without* executing the file | *While* executing in a sandbox | | Examples | PE header, opcode n-grams, strings, byte entropy | API call sequences, network traffic, file-system changes | | Speed | Fast (milliseconds) | Slow (seconds to minutes) | | Evasion resistance | Weak (packers, obfuscation) | Stronger (behaviour visible) | | Computational cost | Low | High | *(3 marks: 1.5 for distinction + 1.5 for tabular comparison.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme Part B carries **internal choice**. The two alternative questions must be solved by *separate* students. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are independent options. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT523]** **"Explain the supervised learning pipeline for malware detection. With a neat block diagram, describe each stage. Discuss the role of feature engineering, classifier choice, and evaluation metrics in determining the success of the system. Illustrate your answer with a practical example such as the EMBER dataset."** #### **Part (a) — Pipeline Stages with Diagram (7 marks)** **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **(i) Sample Collection (1 mark):** Collect a balanced corpus of malicious and benign executables. For the **EMBER dataset** (2018), Endgame Inc. released 1.1M PE files – 900K training + 200K test – labelled using the VirusTotal consensus of 70+ antivirus engines. **(ii) Labelling (0.5 mark):** Each sample receives a binary label $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$ (0 = benign, 1 = malware). Multi-engine consensus voting reduces labelling noise. **(iii) Feature Extraction (1.5 marks):** EMBER uses a **feature vector of dimension 2,381** consisting of: - Parsed PE header fields (e.g., `Machine`, `Subsystem`, `DllCharacteristics`). - Byte histogram (256 bins of byte-value frequency). - String features (mean length, printable string count). - Import address table (IAT) statistics. - Section entropy and section size. **(iv) Train/Validation Split (0.5 mark):** Standard 80/20 stratified split, ensuring the malware/benign ratio is preserved. **(v) Model Training (1.5 marks):** Authors used **LightGBM**, a gradient-boosted decision-tree ensemble, optimising the binary cross-entropy loss: $$ \mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Big[y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\Big] $$ **(vi) Hyperparameter Tuning (1 mark):** 5-fold cross-validation on the training set to choose the number of trees, maximum depth, and learning rate. **(vii) Evaluation (1 mark):** The trained model achieves an ROC-AUC of **0.9991** on the EMBER test set. #### **Part (b) — Practical Example + Critical Discussion (7 marks)** **Practical Example – EMBER Dataset (3.5 marks):** - **Dataset:** EMBER 2018 (Endgame Malware BEnchmark for Research). - **Samples:** 900,000 training + 200,000 test PE files. - **Features:** 2,381-dimensional vector per sample. - **Classifier:** LightGBM (1,000 trees, max depth = 8, learning rate = 0.05). - **Performance:** ROC-AUC ≈ **0.999**, F1-score ≈ **0.98** on the test set. - **Practical impact:** This benchmark enabled reproducible comparison of static-analysis classifiers and is widely cited in academic literature. **Critical Discussion on Feature Engineering, Classifier Choice, Evaluation (3.5 marks):** 1. **Feature Engineering is the most important determinant of success.** EMBER's hand-crafted features (PE header, byte histogram) capture both syntactic and structural cues. Without good features, even deep models fail. *(1 mark)* 2. **Classifier Choice depends on dataset size, feature modality, and latency.** LightGBM was chosen for EMBER because it handles tabular features efficiently and offers fast inference. Deep learning would be preferred for raw byte sequences or behavioural traces. *(1 mark)* 3. **Evaluation Metrics must reflect the security objective.** A 99% *accuracy* is meaningless if the 1% error consists entirely of missed malware (False Negatives). **Recall and F1-score** are the security-critical metrics; in ransomware detection, a False Negative can cost millions. *(1 mark)* 4. **Concept Drift and Adversarial Robustness.** A model trained in 2018 will degrade by 2024 as malware authors adapt. Supervised models must be **retrained regularly** and **augmented with adversarial examples** to remain effective. *(0.5 mark)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECCT523]** **"Discuss three practical case studies where supervised learning has been successfully applied to malware detection. For each case study, describe: (i) the type of malware addressed, (ii) the features used, (iii) the algorithm employed, and (iv) the reported performance. Conclude with a critical evaluation of the limitations of supervised approaches."** #### **Part (a) — Three Practical Case Studies (7 marks)** **Case Study 1: Static PE Malware Classification on EMBER (2.5 marks)** - **(i) Malware type:** Generic Windows PE executables – viruses, trojans, worms, backdoors. - **(ii) Features:** 2,381-dimensional static vector (PE header, byte histogram, string statistics, imports). - **(iii) Algorithm:** LightGBM (gradient-boosted decision trees, 1,000 trees). - **(iv) Performance:** ROC-AUC = **0.9991**; F1-score ≈ 0.98. **Case Study 2: Android Malware Detection on Drebin Dataset (2.5 marks)** - **(i) Malware type:** Android malware (SMS trojans, root exploits, spyware). - **(ii) Features:** Static features extracted from manifest and dex code – requested permissions, API calls, hardware components, filtered intents. Total of **545,000 binary features** per app. - **(iii) Algorithm:** **Linear SVM** (scikit-learn default parameters), chosen for its ability to handle high-dimensional sparse data. - **(iv) Performance:** Detection rate = **93.9%** at a False Positive Rate of **1%**; AUC = 0.97. **Case Study 3: Behavioural Ransomware Detection with LSTM (2 marks)** - **(i) Malware type:** Ransomware families (Locky, Cerber, WannaCry). - **(ii) Features:** Sequential API-call traces captured from sandbox execution – `CryptEncrypt`, `CryptDecrypt`, `WriteFile`, `CreateFile`, `RegSetValueEx` – encoded as integer sequences. - **(iii) Algorithm:** **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** recurrent neural network with 2 hidden layers of 128 units. - **(iv) Performance:** Accuracy = **97.2%**, F1-score = **0.965** on the VirusTotal ransomware subset. #### **Part (b) — Critical Evaluation of Supervised Approaches (7 marks)** **Limitations of Supervised Learning in Malware Detection (each point carries 1.5–2 marks):** 1. **Dependence on Labelled Data (2 marks):** Supervised learning requires massive, high-quality labelled corpora. Labelling is *expensive* – it requires expert analysts or commercial AV engines. The EMBER dataset alone cost Endgame Inc. substantial resources. Emerging malware families may have **no labelled examples**, making pure supervised approaches ineffective. 2. **Adversarial Vulnerability (2 marks):** Malware authors use **adversarial examples** – small, carefully crafted perturbations (e.g., adding benign API calls, inserting dead code, using gradient-masking) that cause the classifier to misclassify malicious files as benign. Recent work (Anderson et al., 2018; Kolosnjaji et al., 2018) has shown that even state-of-the-art MalConv CNNs can be evaded with **append-only attacks** of a few kilobytes. 3. **Concept Drift and Static Models (1.5 marks):** Malware evolves rapidly. A model trained in 2022 may be **obsolete by 2024** because new families, packers, and evasion techniques emerge. Static supervised models require **periodic retraining** – a costly and continuous process. 4. **Class Imbalance (1.5 marks):** In real-world deployment, the majority of files are benign (typically > 99.9%). This **severe imbalance** biases the classifier towards the majority class, causing high false-negative rates unless addressed via SMOTE, class weighting, or threshold tuning. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse Precision with Recall.** Precision answers "of those flagged, how many are real malware?" Recall answers "of all malware, how many did we catch?" Many students mix these up and lose 1–2 marks. > 2. **Do NOT confuse static with dynamic features.** Static = file *not executed*; dynamic = file *executed in sandbox*. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction in Part A. > 3. **Do NOT claim 100% accuracy.** No real malware classifier achieves 100%. Always cite realistic published numbers (F1 ≈ 0.95–0.99 for modern systems). > 4. **Do NOT forget the bias term $b$** in the linear score $z = \mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x} + b$. Forgetting it loses 0.5 mark in derivations. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.** ### Core Concepts - ☐ Supervised learning = training on **labelled** $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ pairs to learn $h_{\theta}$. - ☐ Two classes: $y=1$ (malware) and $y=0$ (benign). - ☐ Feature engineering > algorithm choice (golden rule of applied ML). ### Two Feature Paradigms - ☐ **Static:** PE header, byte n-grams, opcode histogram, strings, entropy. Fast, but evadable by packers. - ☐ **Dynamic:** API calls, network behaviour, file/registry ops. Robust, but slow and sandbox-detectable. ### Key Algorithms - ☐ Logistic Regression (linear, interpretable). - ☐ SVM with RBF kernel (small/medium datasets). - ☐ Random Forest / XGBoost / LightGBM (best for tabular features — *industry default*). - ☐ CNN / LSTM (best for raw bytes and API sequences). - ☐ Naïve Bayes (fast, surprisingly effective baseline). ### Evaluation Metrics — Memorise These - ☐ $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ - ☐ $\text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ - ☐ $\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ - ☐ $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$ - ☐ $\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}$ - ☐ ROC-AUC measures separability across all thresholds. ### Must-Cite Practical Examples - ☐ **EMBER** (static PE, LightGBM, AUC ≈ 0.999). - ☐ **Drebin** (Android, Linear SVM, detection rate 93.9% @ 1% FPR). - ☐ **MalConv** (raw bytes, 1-D CNN, first end-to-end deep malware classifier). - ☐ **CNN/LSTM for ransomware** (behavioural API traces, F1 ≈ 0.97). ### Limitations to Mention in Every Answer - ☐ Labelled data is expensive and lags behind new threats. - ☐ Adversarial evasion attacks. - ☐ Concept drift requires retraining. - ☐ Class imbalance biases the classifier. - ☐ Zero-day malware often has no training samples. ### High-Yield Equations (Write from memory) - ☐ Sigmoid: $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ - ☐ Binary cross-entropy loss. - ☐ F1-score formula. - ☐ Bayes' theorem (for Naïve Bayes derivations).

topics.title: Introduction to Unsupervised Learning Techniques for Anomaly Detection: Overview of Unsupervised Learning - Clustering and Anomaly Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT523 - AI IN CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Unsupervised Learning Techniques for Anomaly Detection: Overview of Unsupervised Learning - Clustering and Anomaly Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Unsupervised Learning

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone)**
> *Unsupervised Learning is a class of Machine Learning algorithms that infer patterns, structure, or groupings from input data **without access to labeled outputs or target classes**. The algorithm must discover the underlying distribution, clusters, or low-dimensional manifold directly from the feature space of the observations.*

Mathematically, given an unlabeled dataset:
$$X = \{x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(m)}\} \quad \text{where} \quad x^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$$

the objective is to learn a mapping $f : X \rightarrow Y_{\text{latent}}$, where $Y_{\text{latent}}$ is a **hidden representation** (cluster ID, projection coordinates, density score, etc.) that the model itself constructs.

The defining contrast with supervised learning is the **absence of a ground-truth label vector** $\mathbf{y} = \{y^{(1)}, y^{(2)}, \ldots, y^{(m)}\}$.

| Property | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| Input | $(X, y)$ | $X$ only |
| Goal | Learn $f(X) \rightarrow y$ | Learn $f(X) \rightarrow$ latent structure |
| Feedback | Loss vs. true label | Internal metrics only (e.g., inertia, silhouette) |
| Cyber Use | Malware classification (known families) | Zero-day anomaly / unknown threat discovery |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Stranger in a Dark Library"**
> Imagine you are dropped into a **completely dark library** containing thousands of books in many languages. You cannot read the titles (no labels), but you start feeling the **size, weight, and paper texture**. Gradually, you group books by similarity — heavy leather-bound volumes in one pile, thin paperbacks in another, picture books in a third. Without anyone telling you the categories, you performed **unsupervised clustering**. Now, the librarian places one oddly shaped metal tablet among the books. It does not fit any cluster. That is an **anomaly** — a sample that breaks the discovered structure.

In cybersecurity terms: the **dark library** is a network traffic log; the **books** are normal connections; the **metal tablet** is a malicious packet, zero-day exploit, or insider threat.

## 1.3 Why Unsupervised Learning is Critical for Cyber Security

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point — The "No Labels" Problem**
> Modern enterprise networks face **thousands of novel threats per day**. Labeling every packet is *impossible* (slow, expensive, requires expert analysts, and attackers actively mutate signatures). Unsupervised methods operate directly on raw traffic and flag **deviations from learned normality** — the only feasible paradigm for *unknown* and *zero-day* attacks.

**Engineering Utility in Cyber Defense:**
1. **Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS):** Cluster flows, mark points far from all centroids.
2. **User & Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA):** Build per-user baselines; flag behavioral drift.
3. **Malware Triage:** Group unknown binaries by API-call embeddings.
4. **Log Analytics:** Surface rare event sequences in SIEM pipelines.
5. **Fraud Detection:** Flag transactions isolated from learned spending clusters.

**Key Principle:** Unsupervised methods trade *label-dependence* for *statistical surprise* — they answer: *"Does this observation fit the pattern of everything else?"*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Unsupervised Learning Techniques

Unsupervised learning for anomaly detection is broadly divided into three families:

1. **Clustering-Based Methods** — Group similar points; anomalies are points that are distant from all cluster centers, in small clusters, or in low-density regions.
2. **Density-Based Methods** — Estimate the probability density $p(x)$; low $p(x)$ ⇒ anomaly.
3. **Dimensionality Reduction / Reconstruction-Based Methods** — Project to a lower space and attempt reconstruction; high reconstruction error ⇒ anomaly (e.g., PCA, Autoencoders — covered later in the module).

This module focuses on **Clustering-based** techniques, the most intuitive and frequently tested in KTU.

## 2.2 Distance & Similarity Metrics (Foundation of Clustering)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite — Distance Metric Selection**
> Choice of metric changes cluster shape drastically. Always justify your metric based on feature scale and domain meaning.

**1. Euclidean (L2) Distance** — straight-line, sphere-shaped clusters.
$$d_{E}(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left(x_{j} - y_{j}\right)^{2}}$$

**2. Manhattan (L1) Distance** — grid-aligned, diamond-shaped clusters, robust to outliers.
$$d_{M}(x, y) = \sum_{j=1}^{n}\vert x_{j} - y_{j} \vert$$

**3. Minkowski (Lp) Distance** — generalized form.
$$d_{p}(x, y) = \left(\sum_{j=1}^{n}\vert x_{j} - y_{j} \vert^{p}\right)^{1/p}$$

**4. Cosine Similarity** — for directional data (e.g., TF-IDF malware reports).
$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{x \cdot y}{\Vert x \Vert \, \Vert y \Vert} = \frac{\sum_{j} x_{j} y_{j}}{\sqrt{\sum_{j} x_{j}^{2}} \, \sqrt{\sum_{j} y_{j}^{2}}}$$

**5. Mahalanobis Distance** — accounts for feature covariance; elliptical clusters.
$$d_{Maha}(x, y) = \sqrt{(x - y)^{T} \Sigma^{-1} (x - y)}$$

## 2.3 K-Means Clustering — Operational Logic

K-Means partitions $m$ observations into $k$ clusters by iteratively minimizing the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS / Inertia)**:

$$J(\mu, C) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_{i}} \Vert x - \mu_{i} \Vert^{2}$$

where:
- $\mu_{i}$ is the **centroid** of cluster $C_{i}$ computed as the mean vector of its members.
- $C = \{C_{1}, C_{2}, \ldots, C_{k}\}$ is the cluster assignment.

**Algorithmic Steps (Lloyd's Algorithm):**
1. Initialize $k$ centroids (randomly, or via **K-Means++** for better seeding).
2. **Assignment step:** assign each $x^{(i)}$ to nearest centroid based on Euclidean distance.
3. **Update step:** recompute each $\mu_{i}$ as the mean of all points in $C_{i}$.
4. Repeat steps 2–3 until centroids stop moving (convergence) or max iterations reached.
5. **Anomaly score** for a point: $a(x) = \min_{i} \Vert x - \mu_{i} \Vert$.

## 2.4 DBSCAN — Density-Based Spatial Clustering

Unlike K-Means, DBSCAN does not require specifying $k$. It uses two parameters:
- $\epsilon$ — radius of neighborhood.
- $\text{MinPts}$ — minimum number of points required to form a dense region.

Definitions:
- **Core point:** has at least $\text{MinPts}$ points within $\epsilon$-radius.
- **Border point:** within $\epsilon$ of a core point but has fewer than $\text{MinPts}$ neighbors.
- **Noise point (anomaly):** neither core nor border — labeled as outlier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anomaly output of DBSCAN is built-in** — points labeled *noise* are direct anomaly candidates, making DBSCAN exceptionally useful for cyber threat hunting on traffic scatter plots.

## 2.5 Hierarchical Clustering — Agglomerative Variant

- Start with each point as its own cluster.
- Iteratively merge the two closest clusters according to a **linkage criterion**:
  - Single linkage: $\min$ pairwise distance.
  - Complete linkage: $\max$ pairwise distance.
  - Average linkage: mean pairwise distance.
  - Ward's linkage: minimizes increase in WCSS.

Output is a **dendrogram**, which can be cut at any level to obtain $k$ clusters.

## 2.6 Types of Anomalies (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Anomaly Type | Definition | Cyber Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Point (Global) Anomaly** | Single instance far from all others | One login from an unknown geo-IP |
| **Contextual Anomaly** | Normal globally, abnormal in specific context | High CPU usage at 3 AM |
| **Collective Anomaly** | A group of instances is anomalous together | Sequential small DNS queries (DNS tunneling) |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the symbols:** $m$ = no. of samples, $n$ = no. of features, $k$ = no. of clusters, $\mu$ = centroid, $\epsilon$ = DBSCAN radius.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euclidean Distance | $d_{E}(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left(x_{j} - y_{j}\right)^{2}}$ | Same units as features |
| 2 | Manhattan Distance | $d_{M}(x, y) = \sum_{j=1}^{n}\vert x_{j} - y_{j} \vert$ | Same units as features |
| 3 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{\sum x_{j} y_{j}}{\Vert x \Vert \, \Vert y \Vert}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$ |
| 4 | Cosine Distance | $d_{C} = 1 - \cos(\theta)$ | Range $[0, 2]$ |
| 5 | K-Means Objective | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{x \in C_{i}}\Vert x - \mu_{i}\Vert^{2}$ | Minimize (inertia) |
| 6 | Centroid Update | $\mu_{i} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{i}\vert}\sum_{x \in C_{i}} x$ | Mean of cluster |
| 7 | Silhouette Score | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$, higher better |
| 8 | Z-Score (Statistical Anomaly) | $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $\vert z\vert > 3$ ⇒ anomaly |
| 9 | DBSCAN Density Reachability | $\vert N_{\epsilon}(p)\vert \geq \text{MinPts}$ | $N_{\epsilon}$ = $\epsilon$-neighborhood |
| 10 | Mahalanobis Distance | $d_{Maha} = \sqrt{(x - \mu)^{T} \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu)}$ | Covariance-aware |

## 2.8 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering

- **WCSS** is minimized by K-Means to find *compact* clusters — vital for traffic profiling.
- **Silhouette score** is the *only* way to *validate clustering without labels* — KTU loves this in 14-mark answers.
- **Z-score** underlies the simplest possible anomaly detector in SIEM rules.
- **Mahalanobis distance** is used in advanced UEBA where correlated features (login time vs. location) must be modeled jointly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the K-Means Centroid Update Rule

We need to find the value of $\mu_{i}$ that minimizes the cluster's contribution to $J$:

$$J_{i} = \sum_{x \in C_{i}} \Vert x - \mu_{i} \Vert^{2}$$

Expand the squared norm:

$$J_{i} = \sum_{x \in C_{i}} \left( (x - \mu_{i})^{T} (x - \mu_{i}) \right)$$

$$J_{i} = \sum_{x \in C_{i}} \left( x^{T} x - 2 \mu_{i}^{T} x + \mu_{i}^{T} \mu_{i} \right)$$

Take the gradient with respect to $\mu_{i}$ and set it to zero:

$$\nabla_{\mu_{i}} J_{i} = \sum_{x \in C_{i}} \left( -2x + 2\mu_{i} \right) = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow \sum_{x \in C_{i}} \mu_{i} = \sum_{x \in C_{i}} x \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert C_{i} \vert \, \mu_{i} = \sum_{x \in C_{i}} x$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{i} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{i}\vert}\sum_{x \in C_{i}} x}$$

This proves that the **optimal centroid is the arithmetic mean** of all points in the cluster.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Silhouette Score

For a sample $x^{(i)}$ in cluster $C_{I}$:

**Step 1 — Cohesion $a(i)$:** mean distance from $x^{(i)}$ to all other points in the same cluster.

$$a(i) = \frac{1}{\vert C_{I}\vert - 1}\sum_{j \in C_{I}, j \neq i} d(x^{(i)}, x^{(j)})$$

**Step 2 — Separation $b(i)$:** minimum mean distance from $x^{(i)}$ to all points in any *other* cluster.

$$b(i) = \min_{J \neq I}\frac{1}{\vert C_{J}\vert}\sum_{j \in C_{J}} d(x^{(i)}, x^{(j)})$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $s(i) \approx 1$ → well-clustered.
- $s(i) \approx 0$ → on the boundary.
- $s(i) < 0$ → likely assigned to the wrong cluster (anomaly candidate).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a cyber SOC dashboard, plotting the **silhouette of every session** as a bar chart gives analysts an *unsupervised confidence score* per flow.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Z-Score Anomaly Threshold

Assuming features are approximately Gaussian, the standardized score for a sample $x$ is:

$$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

The probability of a value exceeding threshold $t$ under $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ is:

$$P(\vert z \vert > t) = 2 \, \Phi(-t)$$

For $t = 3$:

$$P(\vert z \vert > 3) = 2 \times \Phi(-3) \approx 2 \times 0.00135 = 0.0027$$

So only **0.27%** of normal data exceeds $\vert z \vert = 3$ — the classical **3-sigma rule** used in SIEM thresholds.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — K-Means on Network Traffic

**Dataset (2D feature space: packets/sec, bytes/connection):**

| Point | $x_{1}$ (pkt/s) | $x_{2}$ (B/conn) |
|---|---|---|
| A | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| B | 1.5 | 2.0 |
| C | 2.0 | 1.5 |
| D | 8.0 | 8.0 |
| E | 8.5 | 9.0 |
| F | 9.0 | 8.5 |

**Goal:** $k = 2$ clusters, manual Lloyd's algorithm, identify potential anomaly.

### Iteration 0 — Initialization
Choose centroids $\mu_{1}^{(0)} = A = (1.0, 1.0)$ and $\mu_{2}^{(0)} = D = (8.0, 8.0)$.

### Iteration 1 — Assignment

Compute squared Euclidean distance to each centroid.

| Point | $d^{2}$ to $\mu_{1}$ | $d^{2}$ to $\mu_{2}$ | Assigned to |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0.00 | 98.00 | $\mu_{1}$ |
| B | 1.25 | 86.25 | $\mu_{1}$ |
| C | 1.25 | 72.25 | $\mu_{1}$ |
| D | 98.00 | 0.00 | $\mu_{2}$ |
| E | 113.25 | 1.25 | $\mu_{2}$ |
| F | 105.25 | 1.25 | $\mu_{2}$ |

So $C_{1} = \{A, B, C\}$, $C_{2} = \{D, E, F\}$.

### Iteration 1 — Update Centroids

$$\mu_{1}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(1,1) + (1.5, 2) + (2, 1.5)\right] = (1.50,\ 1.50)$$

$$\mu_{2}^{(1)} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(8, 8) + (8.5, 9) + (9, 8.5)\right] = (8.50,\ 8.50)$$

### Iteration 2 — Re-assignment
Distance ratios confirm cluster membership unchanged → **converged**.

### Anomaly Scoring (using final centroids)

$$a(A) = \sqrt{(1 - 1.5)^{2} + (1 - 1.5)^{2}} = 0.707$$

$$a(E) = \sqrt{(8.5 - 8.5)^{2} + (9 - 8.5)^{2}} = 0.500$$

**Now inject a potential attacker point $Z = (20, 20)$:**

$$a(Z) = \sqrt{(20 - 1.5)^{2} + (20 - 1.5)^{2}} = \sqrt{342.25 + 342.25} \approx 26.17$$

Compare to mean within-cluster distance (≈ 0.61). $a(Z)$ is **~43× larger** → **flagged as anomaly** (e.g., data exfiltration burst).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Spherical clusters, known $k$, large data | K-Means | Fast ($O(mk\,i)$), scalable |
| Arbitrary shapes, unknown $k$, noise expected | DBSCAN | Detects noise natively, no $k$ |
| Hierarchical grouping, small data | Agglomerative | Dendrogram reveals nested threats |
| High-dimensional sparse text (e.g., log keywords) | K-Means + Cosine | Cosine handles direction, not magnitude |
| Need probabilistic anomaly score | Gaussian Mixture Model | Soft assignment, density estimate |
| Multicollinear features | K-Means + Mahalanobis | Accounts for covariance |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Unsupervised Learning for Anomaly Detection

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Unsupervised Learning] --> B[Clustering Based]
    A --> C[Density Based]
    A --> D[Reconstruction Based]
    A --> E[Manifold Learning]

    B --> B1[K Means]
    B --> B2[Hierarchical Agglomerative]
    B --> B3[Fuzzy C Means]
    B --> B4[Spectral Clustering]

    C --> C1[DBSCAN]
    C --> C2[OPTICS]
    C --> C3[Gaussian Mixture Models]

    D --> D1[PCA Reconstruction]
    D --> D2[Autoencoders]
    D --> D3[Isolation Forest]

    E --> E1[t SNE Visualization]
    E --> E2[UMAP]

    B1 --> F[Anomaly Score: distance to centroid]
    C1 --> G[Anomaly Label: noise points]
    D2 --> H[Anomaly Score: reconstruction error]
```

## 4.2 End-to-End Cyber Anomaly Detection Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Raw Network Traffic] --> B[Feature Extraction]
    B --> C[Normalization and Scaling]
    C --> D[Unsupervised Model Training]
    D --> E[Cluster Assignments]
    E --> F[Anomaly Score Computation]
    F --> G{Threshold Check}
    G -->|Score Above Threshold| H[Alert SOC Analyst]
    G -->|Score Below Threshold| I[Mark Normal]
    H --> J[Incident Response]
    I --> K[Continue Monitoring]
```

## 4.3 K-Means Lloyd's Algorithm (Operational Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Initialize K Centroids] --> B[Assign Each Point to Nearest Centroid]
    B --> C[Recompute Centroid as Cluster Mean]
    C --> D{Converged or Max Iterations}
    D -->|No| B
    D -->|Yes| E[Output Final Clusters]
    E --> F[Compute Anomaly Score: Min Distance to Any Centroid]
    F --> G[Flag Outliers Above Threshold]
```

## 4.4 DBSCAN Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[For Each Point P] --> B{Number of Pts in Epsilon Neighborhood >= MinPts}
    B -->|Yes| C[Mark P as Core Point]
    B -->|No| D{Neighbor of Existing Core Point}
    D -->|Yes| E[Mark P as Border Point]
    D -->|No| F[Mark P as Noise or Anomaly]
    C --> G[Form or Extend Cluster]
    E --> G
    F --> H[Anomaly Candidate for SOC Review]
```

## 4.5 Anomaly Type Classification (Cyber Context)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Cyber Anomaly] --> B[Point Anomaly]
    A --> C[Contextual Anomaly]
    A --> D[Collective Anomaly]

    B --> B1[Single Login from Unusual Geo Location]
    B --> B2[Sudden Spike in Outbound Data]

    C --> C1[Encrypted Traffic at 3 AM]
    C --> C2[Admin Privilege Use During Off Hours]

    D --> D1[Sequential Small DNS Queries for Tunneling]
    D --> D2[Coordinated Low Rate DDoS Pattern]
```

## 4.6 Comparative Method Block Diagram (Engineering Decision Aid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[New Cyber Dataset] --> B{Known K?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Large Data?]
    B -->|No| D[Noise Expected?]
    C -->|Yes| E[K Means]
    C -->|No| F[K Medoids]
    D -->|Yes| G[DBSCAN]
    D -->|No| H[Hierarchical]
    E --> I[Anomaly by Centroid Distance]
    G --> J[Anomaly by Noise Label]
    H --> K[Anomaly by Dendrogram Cut]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]
**Define unsupervised learning. Why is it preferred over supervised learning in anomaly detection for cybersecurity applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Unsupervised learning is a class of ML algorithms that learn patterns, structure, or groupings from **input data without using labeled outputs**. The model autonomously discovers the underlying distribution, clusters, or low-dimensional representation.
- **Why preferred in cybersecurity (1.5 marks):**
  1. **Zero-day and unknown attacks** lack historical labels — supervised classifiers cannot recognize them.
  2. **Labeling is expensive** and requires expert SOC analysts; manual tagging cannot scale to terabytes of daily traffic.
  3. Unsupervised methods build a *baseline of normality* and flag **statistical deviations**, making them robust against novel threats whose signatures have never been seen.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]
**Differentiate between point, contextual, and collective anomalies. Give one cybersecurity example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Type | Definition (0.5 mark) | Cyber Example (0.5 mark) |
|---|---|---|
| **Point Anomaly** | A single data instance deviates sharply from the rest of the dataset. | A single login attempt from an IP in a sanctioned country at 2 PM local time. |
| **Contextual Anomaly** | Instance is anomalous only in a specific context (time, location, user role) but normal otherwise. | A user downloading 5 GB of files at 3 AM, although their daytime traffic is normal. |
| **Collective Anomaly** | A set of related instances is anomalous together, even if individual instances appear normal. | A coordinated sequence of low-rate DNS queries indicative of DNS tunneling. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Format)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losses**
> 1. Writing definitions without **mathematical formulation** loses 2–3 marks in 14-mark questions. Always include an equation (e.g., K-Means objective, distance metric).
> 2. Listing algorithms without showing **why one is chosen over another** for a cyber use-case loses application-level marks.
> 3. Skipping the **anomaly scoring step** after clustering — students often stop at "cluster formed" without explaining *how* anomalies are flagged.
> 4. Using `|` inside markdown tables breaks the table — examiners literally cannot read your answer. Always use `\vert` in LaTeX.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Module 2, Internal Choice Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **K-Means clustering algorithm** in detail. State its objective function and derive the centroid update rule. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss how **DBSCAN** differs from K-Means. With a suitable diagram, explain how DBSCAN identifies core, border, and noise points. Apply DBSCAN intuition to detect a network anomaly. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) K-Means Algorithm (7 marks):**

**K-Means** is a centroid-based partitioning algorithm that divides $m$ observations into $k$ non-overlapping clusters.

**Objective Function (2 marks):**
$$J(\mu, C) = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{x \in C_{i}}\Vert x - \mu_{i}\Vert^{2}$$

**Steps of Lloyd's Algorithm (3 marks):**
1. Initialize $k$ centroids $\mu_{1}, \mu_{2}, \ldots, \mu_{k}$ (random or K-Means++).
2. **Assignment:** For each $x^{(i)}$, assign it to cluster with nearest centroid using Euclidean distance $d_{E}$.
3. **Update:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of its cluster members.
4. Iterate steps 2 and 3 until centroids stabilize or max iterations reached.

**Derivation of Centroid Update (2 marks):**
Setting $\nabla_{\mu_{i}} J_{i} = 0$ yields:

$$\mu_{i}^{*} = \frac{1}{\vert C_{i}\vert}\sum_{x \in C_{i}} x$$

**Anomaly Usage:** $a(x) = \min_{i}\Vert x - \mu_{i}\Vert^{2}$; high $a(x)$ ⇒ anomaly.

---

**(b) DBSCAN vs K-Means + Anomaly Application (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Property | K-Means | DBSCAN |
|---|---|---|
| Requires $k$ in advance | Yes | No |
| Cluster shape | Spherical | Arbitrary |
| Handles noise | Poor (forces assignment) | Native (labels as noise) |
| Density sensitivity | None | Two parameters: $\epsilon$, MinPts |

**DBSCAN Point Categories (3 marks):**
- **Core point:** has $\geq$ MinPts neighbors within $\epsilon$.
- **Border point:** within $\epsilon$ of a core, but has $<$ MinPts neighbors.
- **Noise point:** neither — **directly output as anomaly**.

**Cyber Application (2 marks):**
On a 2D scatter of (packet_size, inter_arrival_time), DBSCAN forms dense clusters of normal flows. A low-rate port-scan appears as scattered isolated points in low-density regions — automatically labeled as *noise* by DBSCAN and forwarded to the SOC. The algorithm thus provides **unsupervised, signature-free detection** of reconnaissance activity.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Module 2, Internal Choice Pattern]

**(a)** With suitable mathematical formulation, explain the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** and the **Silhouette Score** as unsupervised cluster validation metrics. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the three types of anomalies (point, contextual, collective) with **two real-world cybersecurity scenarios** for each. Discuss how clustering-based unsupervised methods can detect each type. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) WCSS and Silhouette Score (7 marks):**

**WCSS / Inertia (3 marks):**
$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{x \in C_{i}}\Vert x - \mu_{i}\Vert^{2}$$

WCSS is **minimized** by K-Means. To choose the optimal $k$, plot $J$ vs. $k$ and apply the **elbow method** — the point where the rate of decrease sharply changes is the optimal trade-off between compactness and complexity.

**Silhouette Score (3 marks):**

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i),\ b(i)\}}$$

where:
- $a(i)$ = mean intra-cluster distance (cohesion).
- $b(i)$ = minimum mean inter-cluster distance (separation).

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
- $s(i) \rightarrow 1$ ⇒ well-clustered.
- $s(i) \rightarrow 0$ ⇒ on boundary.
- $s(i) < 0$ ⇒ likely misassigned — anomaly candidate.

**Engineering Note:** Silhouette is the only internal metric that does not require ground-truth labels, making it ideal for validating cyber traffic clusters in production SIEM systems.

---

**(b) Anomaly Types and Detection (7 marks):**

**Point Anomaly (2 marks):**
A single event deviates from the global norm. *Examples:* (i) a single 50 GB outbound transfer from a workstation that normally sends 100 MB/day, (ii) a single login from an IP in a hostile region. **Detection:** Compute $\min_{i}\Vert x - \mu_{i}\Vert$ — points lying far from every cluster centroid are flagged.

**Contextual Anomaly (2 marks):**
Normal globally, abnormal in specific context. *Examples:* (i) a system administrator logging in at 4 AM (time-contextual), (ii) a finance user suddenly accessing HR records (role-contextual). **Detection:** Train separate K-Means models per context window (e.g., hourly, per-role) — divergence from the role-specific cluster signals anomaly.

**Collective Anomaly (3 marks):**
A sequence or group of related events is anomalous even if individual events look normal. *Examples:* (i) sequential slow-rate DNS queries encoding exfiltrated data via DNS tunneling, (ii) coordinated low-rate SYN packets from a botnet forming a low-and-slow DDoS. **Detection:** Feature engineering on *n-grams* of events; cluster on aggregated feature vectors representing groups of events — collective outliers form small or isolated clusters.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Print This Box!**

### Core Concepts
- **Unsupervised learning** = learning from $X$ only, no $y$ (no labels).
- **Three families:** Clustering, Density, Reconstruction.
- **Why for cyber:** zero-day threats have no labels.

### Must-Know Algorithms
- **K-Means:** minimize WCSS; centroids = cluster means; anomaly = distance to nearest centroid.
- **DBSCAN:** parameters $\epsilon$ and MinPts; outputs *noise* points as anomalies directly.
- **Hierarchical:** dendrogram; merge by linkage (single, complete, average, Ward).

### Must-Know Distance Metrics
- Euclidean (L2), Manhattan (L1), Cosine (for text), Mahalanobis (for correlated features).
- Use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX, never `|x|`, inside markdown tables.

### Anomaly Types
- **Point:** single global deviation.
- **Contextual:** anomaly in a specific time/role/location context.
- **Collective:** group of events anomalous together.

### Validation Without Labels
- **WCSS (inertia)** — elbow method for choosing $k$.
- **Silhouette score** $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ — best unsupervised cluster-quality metric.
- **Z-score** — $\vert z \vert > 3$ ⇒ 0.27% false positive under Gaussian assumption.

### Common Exam Pitfalls (Avoid These!)
- ✗ Forgetting to write the *objective function* when describing K-Means.
- ✗ Stating "DBSCAN is density-based" without defining $\epsilon$ and MinPts.
- ✗ Confusing *point* anomaly with *contextual* anomaly in 3-mark questions.
- ✗ Not linking algorithms to *cyber use-cases* — application marks require SOC/network examples.
- ✗ Using `|` for absolute value in markdown tables — breaks formatting.

### Quick Memory Hooks
- **"K-Means = Compact & Round"** → centroid-based, Euclidean, spherical.
- **"DBSCAN = Shape-free & Noisy"** → density-based, $\epsilon$-ball, noise = anomaly.
- **"Silhouette = -1 to +1"** → cohesion vs. separation ratio.
- **"Z = 3 sigma"** → 99.7% rule for Gaussian anomaly threshold.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: Unsupervised Learning in Detecting Insider Threats (e.g., Detecting Insider Trading in Financial Institutions)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT523 - AI IN CYBER SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Case Study: Unsupervised Learning in Detecting Insider Threats (e.g., Detecting Insider Trading in Financial Institutions)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unsupervised Learning for Insider Threat Detection** is a category of machine learning where the model is trained on **unlabeled** telemetry data (network logs, trade records, access logs, communication patterns) to discover hidden structures, clusters, and anomalies that may represent malicious or anomalous behaviour by trusted internal actors, without relying on a pre-curated ground-truth dataset of past incidents.

In the specific context of **detecting insider trading in financial institutions**, unsupervised learning is used to profile the normal trading, communication, and access behaviour of employees (traders, analysts, portfolio managers) and flag statistically significant deviations that may indicate illegal use of Material Non-Public Information (MNPI).

### Key Terminology Anchors

| Term | KTU-Standard Definition |
|------|--------------------------|
| **Insider Threat** | A security risk that originates from people within the organization (employees, contractors) who have authorized access to confidential systems or data. |
| **Insider Trading** | The illegal practice of trading a public company's stock or other securities by someone who has access to MNPI about the company. |
| **Anomaly Score** | A scalar value produced by an unsupervised model indicating how "different" a data point is from the learned normal distribution. |
| **MNPI (Material Non-Public Information)** | Information about a company that has not been disclosed to the public and could influence an investor's decision to buy/sell securities. |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | The proportion of legitimate users/alerts incorrectly classified as malicious by the model. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Emphasis**: Insider threats account for approximately **60% of data breaches** in the financial sector according to industry reports. The "unsupervised" aspect is critical because **labelled datasets of insider-trading incidents are extremely scarce** due to legal confidentiality and the small number of confirmed cases.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of unsupervised insider-threat detection as a **security guard who has never seen a thief before**.

Imagine a museum with 1000 visitors per day. The guard has no list of "known shoplifters," no photos, and no prior arrests to reference. Instead, the guard simply **watches patterns for weeks**:

- Normal visitors walk in, look at exhibits for 30–60 minutes, take a few photos, and leave.
- Normal visitors keep their hands visible and stay within roped-off areas.
- A potential thief might: linger near a specific exhibit, repeatedly check the guard's position, return to the same room multiple times, carry a large bag, and stay until closing time.

The guard builds an **internal mental model of "normal"** and anything that breaks the pattern triggers a quiet alert. This is exactly how an unsupervised model works:

- **Data points** = employees' daily activity records.
- **"Normal" baseline** = clustered patterns of routine behaviour.
- **Anomaly score** = how far a current activity deviates from that learned baseline.
- **Alert** = points that fall outside all dense clusters (i.e., potential insider).

> [!TIP]
> **The "No Teacher" Principle**: Unlike supervised learning (where the guard is shown pictures of thieves and told "this is bad"), unsupervised learning lets the model *invent* its own definition of suspicious by measuring the statistical "shape" of the data itself.

## 1.3 Why Unsupervised Learning Specifically?

In financial institutions, supervised learning is often impractical for three reasons:

1. **Class Imbalance / Data Scarcity**: Confirmed insider-trading cases may be only 0.001% of all trades — too few to train a balanced classifier.
2. **Concept Drift**: Trading strategies evolve with markets; yesterday's fraud pattern may not match today's.
3. **Adversarial Adaptation**: Sophisticated insiders deliberately mimic normal behaviour to evade detection; supervised models trained on known attacks fail against novel attack vectors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unsupervised learning solves all three** by treating "anomaly = deviation from collective norm" rather than "anomaly = matches a known bad signature."

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline (How It Works in a Bank)

The end-to-end unsupervised insider-threat detection pipeline in a financial institution follows five structured stages:

### Stage 1 — Data Ingestion (Multi-Source Telemetry)

The system fuses heterogeneous data sources to build a holistic user profile:

- **Trade Logs**: Order type, ticker symbol, volume, timestamp, counter-party, profit/loss.
- **Communication Records**: Email metadata, chat logs (Bloomberg terminal, internal Slack/Teams), frequency of contact with specific external addresses.
- **File Access Logs**: Documents opened, downloaded, printed, or shared on SharePoint/Confluence.
- **Network Logs**: VPN logins, geolocation of logins, working-hour patterns, device fingerprints.
- **HR / Role Data**: Department, seniority, recent promotions, resignation status.

### Stage 2 — Feature Engineering (Behavioural Vectorization)

Each user is converted into a feature vector $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ where $d$ is the number of behavioural features. Typical engineered features include:

- **Volume features**: trades/day, total value traded, off-hours activity ratio.
- **Diversity features**: number of unique counterparties, sector concentration.
- **Temporal features**: entropy of trade timing, deviation from scheduled hours.
- **Communication features**: graph centrality in email network, frequency of contact with external domains.
- **Text-derived features**: TF-IDF or embedding-based vectorization of email content (e.g., sentiment, presence of MNPI keywords like "merger", "earnings", "confidential").

### Stage 3 — Model Training (Clustering / Density Estimation)

The model learns the underlying structure of the multivariate feature space $\mathcal{X} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$. The assumption (called the **low-density anomaly assumption**) is that normal data points lie in **high-density regions**, while anomalies lie in **low-density regions**.

### Stage 4 — Anomaly Scoring

Each new observation $\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}$ receives a score $s(\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}})$. If $s$ exceeds a threshold $\tau$, the user/activity is flagged for human review by a **Compliance Officer** or **Surveillance Analyst**.

### Stage 5 — Human-in-the-Loop Validation

Flagged cases are reviewed. Confirmed cases feed back into a *labelled* dataset that may later be used to train a **supervised refinement model** — this is called the **semi-supervised** or **active learning** upgrade path.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note**: The pipeline is *not* fully autonomous. Regulatory frameworks (SEC Rule 17a-4, FINRA Rule 3110) require human review of all surveillance alerts before any action is taken.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations form the mathematical core of unsupervised insider-threat detection.

### Table 1: Distance, Density, and Scoring Metrics

| # | Concept | Equation | Notation & Units |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Euclidean Distance** between two feature vectors | $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{d} (x_{ik} - x_{jk})^2}$ | $\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j \in \mathbb{R}^d$; unitless after standardization |
| 2 | **Cosine Similarity** (for trade-direction vectors) | $\cos(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \dfrac{\mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}_j}{\Vert \mathbf{x}_i \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{x}_j \Vert}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$; dimensionless |
| 3 | **Mahalanobis Distance** (accounting for feature correlations) | $D_M(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$ | $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ = mean, $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ = covariance matrix |
| 4 | **K-Means Objective (WCSS)** | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}(c_i = k) \cdot \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2$ | $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ = centroid of cluster $k$ |
| 5 | **Silhouette Score** for cluster validity | $s_i = \dfrac{b_i - a_i}{\max(a_i, b_i)}$ | $a_i$ = mean intra-cluster distance; $b_i$ = mean nearest-cluster distance; range $[-1, 1]$ |
| 6 | **Local Outlier Factor (LOF)** | $\text{LOF}_k(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}) \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x}' \in \mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x})} \dfrac{\text{lrd}(\mathbf{x}')}{\text{lrd}(\mathbf{x})}$ | $\mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x})$ = $k$-nearest neighbours; lrd = local reachability density |
| 7 | **Isolation Forest Anomaly Score** | $s(\mathbf{x}, n) = 2^{-\dfrac{E(h(\mathbf{x}))}{c(n)}}$ | $E(h(\mathbf{x}))$ = mean path length; $c(n) = 2 H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n}$ |
| 8 | **Reconstruction Error (Autoencoder)** | $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}) = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}} \Vert^2$ | $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ = decoder output; threshold $\tau$ on $\mathcal{L}$ flags anomalies |
| 9 | **DB-SCAN Density Condition** | $\vert \mathcal{N}_\epsilon(\mathbf{x}) \vert \geq \text{minPts}$ | $\mathcal{N}_\epsilon(\mathbf{x})$ = points within radius $\epsilon$ |
| 10 | **Information Entropy (Communication Diversity)** | $H(\mathbf{x}) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $p_i$ = probability of the $i$-th communication/counterparty |

### Boundary Conditions & Practical Constraints

- **$K$ in K-Means**: chosen by the **elbow method** (plot WCSS vs. $K$) or **silhouette analysis**.
- **$\epsilon$ in DBSCAN**: typically chosen via the **k-distance graph** at the inflection point.
- **Threshold $\tau$ in Autoencoder**: set at the 95th–99th percentile of reconstruction error on a *clean training set*.
- **Standardization**: all features must be **z-score normalized** ($z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$) before distance-based models to prevent high-magnitude features from dominating.

## 2.3 Why Each Step Matters in Real Engineering

- **Distance metrics** translate abstract behaviour into numeric space, enabling algorithms to compare users.
- **Clustering** groups users with similar roles (e.g., "day traders," "long-term portfolio managers") so anomalies are evaluated *within* a peer group, not globally.
- **Density estimation** is critical because financial data is heavily skewed (e.g., a few traders execute 80% of volume).
- **Reconstruction error** for autoencoders captures **non-linear** behavioural signatures that linear models miss.
- **Human-in-the-loop** is mandated by law; the system *assists* compliance teams, it does not replace them.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — K-Means Clustering for Trader Segmentation

**Problem Setup**: A bank has $n = 1000$ traders. Each trader's daily behaviour is encoded as a feature vector $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^2$ where:
- $x_{i1}$ = number of trades per day
- $x_{i2}$ = average trade size in USD (log-scaled)

We wish to segment traders into $K = 3$ behavioural clusters using K-Means.

### Step 1 — Initialize Centroids

Select $K = 3$ initial centroids $\{\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(0)}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_3^{(0)}\}$ randomly from the dataset.

### Step 2 — Assignment Step

For each trader $\mathbf{x}_i$, assign to the nearest centroid by Euclidean distance:

$$
c_i^{(t)} = \arg\min_{k \in \{1, 2, 3\}} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t)} \Vert^2
$$

This is the hard assignment: each trader belongs to exactly one cluster.

### Step 3 — Update Step

Recompute each centroid as the mean of all points assigned to it:

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{i : c_i = k} \mathbf{x}_i
$$

where $C_k = \{i : c_i^{(t)} = k\}$ is the set of indices in cluster $k$.

### Step 4 — Iterate

Repeat Steps 2–3 until the within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS) converges:

$$
J^{(t+1)} - J^{(t)} < \epsilon
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Anomaly Distance

After convergence, for any new trader $\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}$, compute the minimum distance to any centroid:

$$
d_{\text{anomaly}}(\mathbf{x}_{\text{new}}) = \min_{k=1,\dots,K} \Vert \mathbf{x}_{\text{new}} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert
$$

If $d_{\text{anomaly}} > \tau_{\text{cluster}}$ (e.g., 3 standard deviations above the mean cluster distance), flag as anomalous.

### Convergence Proof (Sketch)

The K-Means objective $J$ is non-increasing at every iteration because:
- **Assignment step** minimizes $J$ with fixed centroids.
- **Update step** minimizes $J$ with fixed assignments (centroid is the unique minimizer of squared distance).

Since $J \geq 0$ and the algorithm only visits a finite number of distinct partition states, it must converge in finite steps.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Isolation Forest Anomaly Score

**Motivation**: For insider trading, a trader's trades are not point anomalies but **contextual anomalies** — a high volume of trades is normal for a high-frequency trader but anomalous for a back-office analyst. Isolation Forest handles this elegantly.

### Step 1 — Build Isolation Trees (iTrees)

For $t = 1, \dots, T$:
- Sample a subset $\mathcal{X}' \subset \mathcal{X}$ of size $\psi$.
